<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# Section 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Question 1 (3 Marks)**
**[KTU University Exam – Model Question, Module 1]**

**Q: Define the term "Mapping" in the context of UI/UX design. Differentiate between natural mapping and conventional mapping with one example each.**

**Model Answer:**

**Mapping** is the relationship between a user interface control (such as a button, slider, or icon) and the effect or result that the control produces when activated. A well-mapped interface allows users to predict outcomes without conscious reasoning.

**Natural Mapping:** It exploits **spatial or physical analogies** so that the relationship is self-evident. 
*Example:* A volume slider where moving the handle to the right increases the sound (mirrors a physical fader).

**Conventional (Cultural) Mapping:** It relies on **learned conventions** or widely accepted standards in a particular culture or industry. 
*Example:* A **trash can icon** universally represents a "Delete" action in software interfaces, even though it has no physical analog to deletion.

> [!NOTE]
> **Valuation Tip:** Award **1 mark** for defining mapping, **1 mark** for natural mapping with example, **1 mark** for conventional mapping with example. Avoid writing vague definitions — use the **control-effect** terminology explicitly.

---

### **Question 2 (3 Marks)**
**[KTU University Exam – Model Question, Module 1]**

**Q: Explain Krug's principle "Don't make me think" in relation to interface mappings. State any two design heuristics derived from this principle.**

**Model Answer:**

Krug's principle **"Don't make me think"** asserts that a well-designed interface should be **self-evident** — users should be able to understand and use it **without expending mental effort** to decode controls, labels, or navigation.

In relation to mappings, this means the **relationship between a control and its effect must be immediately obvious**. If a user has to pause and reason about what a button does, the mapping has failed.

**Two Design Heuristics:**

1. **Use Established Conventions:** If a convention exists for a particular UI element (e.g., logo in top-left = home, shopping cart in top-right = cart), use it. Conventions reduce cognitive load by leveraging the user's prior experience.

2. **Make Clickable Elements Look Clickable:** Apply visual signifiers such as color, underline, button shadows, or hover states. If something is interactive, it **must signal** its interactivity. Conversely, non-interactive text should not appear clickable.

> [!NOTE]
> **Valuation Tip:** Mentioning Krug by name and referencing specific heuristics (conventions, visual signifiers, clickable cues) scores full marks. Generic statements like "make it user-friendly" will **not** earn marks.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

### **Question A (14 Marks)**
**[KTU University Exam – Model Question, Module 1]**

#### **Part (a) — 7 Marks**

**Q: Discuss in detail the concept of "Getting the Mappings Right" as explained by Steve Krug. Explain the three categories of mappings proposed by Don Norman with suitable UI examples for each.**

**Model Answer:**

**Introduction to "Getting the Mappings Right":**

In his book *Don't Make Me Think: A Common Sense Approach to Web Usability*, Steve Krug emphasizes that a usable web page must be **self-explanatory**. Users should not have to stop, investigate, or puzzle over the meaning of interface elements. "Getting the mappings right" is the practice of ensuring that **every interactive element on a page clearly signals what it does and what will happen when the user interacts with it**.

A mapping, in this context, is the **correspondence between a control (a button, icon, or link) and the effect it produces (navigation, data submission, state change)**. When this correspondence is intuitive, the user acts **without thinking**. When it is ambiguous, the user pauses, which is a usability failure.

**Don Norman's Three Categories of Mappings:**

**1. Natural Mapping:**
A natural mapping uses **spatial, physical, or anatomical analogies** to make the control-effect relationship self-evident. The user does not need to learn anything new because the mapping exploits pre-existing mental models built from real-world experience.

*UI Example 1:* A **light bulb toggle** in a smart home app where tapping the bulb icon turns the light on and off — the icon physically represents the device it controls.

*UI Example 2:* A **map application** where dragging the map to the right moves the visible area to the right, mirroring the physical act of sliding a paper map.

*UI Example 3:* A **temperature control** in a thermostat app where the dial is rotated clockwise to increase temperature, matching the behavior of a real-world thermostat knob.

**2. Cultural / Conventional Mapping:**
A conventional mapping relies on **learned signifiers and standards** that are widely accepted within a particular culture, platform, or industry. The user must have **prior exposure** to recognize the convention, but once learned, it becomes intuitive.

*UI Example 1:* The **floppy disk icon** for "Save" in word processors — universally recognized despite floppy disks being technologically obsolete.

*UI Example 2:* The **three horizontal lines (hamburger menu)** for navigation in mobile apps — a modern convention that signals a hidden menu.

*UI Example 3:* The **magnifying glass icon** for "Search" — recognized across search engines, e-commerce sites, and operating systems.

**3. Arbitrary Mapping:**
An arbitrary mapping has **no inherent visual or logical relationship** between the control and its effect. The user must rely on **labels, memory, or instruction manuals** to use the control correctly. This is the **least desirable** mapping type in UI design.

*UI Example 1:* **Keyboard shortcuts** like `Ctrl+Alt+Del` for system interruption in Windows — arbitrary but standardized through decades of use.

*UI Example 2:* A **cryptic code** like "X9Z2" used as a link to advanced settings — the user has no way to predict the effect from the label.

*UI Example 3:* **"Mystery meat navigation"** — buttons with no labels or recognizable icons, forcing users to hover or click to discover their function.

**Conclusion:**

Effective UI design prioritizes **natural mappings**, falls back on **conventional mappings** when natural ones are not feasible, and **avoids arbitrary mappings** whenever possible. Adhering to this hierarchy ensures compliance with Krug's foundational principle of usability.

#### **Part (b) — 7 Marks**

**Q: Analyze the following e-commerce website interface using the Mapping Quality Index (MQI) framework. Identify at least five controls, classify their mappings, and suggest improvements.**

**Interface Description (Hypothetical):**
- A search bar with a magnifying glass icon.
- A "🛒" cart icon in the top-right corner.
- A red "BUY" button at the bottom of each product card.
- A small "?" icon next to the price.
- A small underlined text "T&C" at the footer.

**Model Answer:**

**Step 1 — Identify the Controls and Effects:**

| Control | Visual Cue | Effect | Mapping Type | Score |
|---------|------------|--------|--------------|-------|
| Search bar with magnifying glass | Icon + placeholder text | Opens search results | Cultural (universal) | 3 |
| Cart icon (🛒) | Icon only, top-right | Opens shopping cart | Cultural (platform standard) | 3 |
| Red "BUY" button | Text + button styling | Purchases product immediately | Labeled Arbitrary (but concerning UX) | 2 |
| "?" icon next to price | Icon only | Shows price breakdown | Pure Arbitrary (ambiguous) | 1 |
| "T&C" underlined text | Underline (signals link) | Opens Terms and Conditions | Conventional (legal standard) | 3 |

**Step 2 — Compute the MQI:**

$$\begin{aligned}
M_{interface} &= \frac{3 + 3 + 2 + 1 + 3}{5} = \frac{12}{5} = 2.4
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 3 — Verdict:**

$1.5 \leq 2.4 < 2.5$ → **"Needs Improvement"**

**Step 4 — Design Improvements:**

1. **"?" Icon** → Replace with a clearer label such as **"Price Details"** or a tooltip on hover. This converts a pure arbitrary mapping (score 1) to a labeled arbitrary mapping (score 2).

2. **"BUY" Button** → Rename to **"Buy Now"** and add a secondary **"Add to Cart"** button. This gives the user a choice and makes the action's effect explicit (score 2 → 3).

3. **Cart Icon** → Add a **red badge** showing the number of items in the cart. This adds a signifier and increases discoverability.

4. **Search Bar** → Add a **clear "Search" button** next to the input field for users who do not know about the Enter-key convention.

5. **"T&C" Link** → Expand to **"Terms & Conditions"** to make it accessible to first-time users.

**Step 5 — Recomputed MQI After Improvements:**

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{New Scores: } & [3, 4, 3, 2, 3] \\
M_{interface} &= \frac{3 + 4 + 3 + 2 + 3}{5} = \frac{15}{5} = 3.0
\end{aligned}$$

**New Verdict:** $2.5 \leq 3.0 < 3.5$ → **"Good"**

**Conclusion:** The redesign elevates the interface from "Needs Improvement" to "Good" by applying Norman's hierarchy of mappings and Krug's usability heuristics.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**
**[KTU University Exam – Model Question, Module 1]**

#### **Part (a) — 7 Marks**

**Q: Explain the difference between Affordance, Signifier, and Mapping. How do these three concepts work together to create a usable interface? Illustrate with a real-world example.**

**Model Answer:**

**Conceptual Definitions:**

**1. Affordance:**
An affordance is the **perceived property of an object that suggests how it can be used**. Coined by perceptual psychologist J.J. Gibson and later popularized by Don Norman, affordance describes what actions are *possible* with an object.

*Example:* A **door handle** affords pulling. A **flat plate** on a door affords pushing. The shape itself communicates the intended action.

In UI: A **button** affords pressing due to its raised, rectangular shape. A **slider thumb** affords dragging.

**2. Signifier:**
A signifier is a **visible cue or signal** that communicates where the user should act. Affordances may exist, but if they are not visible, signifiers make them perceivable.

*Example:* The **text "Pull"** on a door is a signifier that communicates the affordance. In web design, an **underline on a link** is a signifier that signals clickability.

In UI: A **cursor change** when hovering over a button is a signifier. A **color change** on hover is a signifier. A **tooltip** with the text "Click to submit" is a signifier.

**3. Mapping:**
A mapping is the **relationship between a control and the effect it produces**. While affordance tells you *what you can do* and signifier tells you *where to act*, mapping tells you *what will happen*.

*Example:* The mapping between a **TV remote's volume-up button (marked with a "+")** and the effect of increased sound.

In UI: The mapping between a **"Send" button** and the effect of submitting a message.

**How They Work Together — A Real-World Example: A Microwave Oven**

Consider a modern microwave oven with a digital touch panel:

- **Affordance:** The flat touch panel **affords touching**. The buttons are recessed or outlined, suggesting they can be pressed.
- **Signifier:** The buttons have **icons and labels** (e.g., a snowflake for "Defrost," a clock for "Timer," a power symbol for "Start"). These are the visible cues.
- **Mapping:** Pressing the **"Start" button (with the play triangle ▶ icon)** begins the cooking process. The control-effect relationship is clear because the play symbol is a **cultural convention** borrowed from media players.

When all three work in harmony, the user interacts **without thinking** — Krug's ideal. If any one is missing or weak, usability degrades.

**Conclusion:**
- **Affordance** = Possibility
- **Signifier** = Communication
- **Mapping** = Relationship

A usable interface requires **clear affordances, visible signifiers, and correct mappings**, working in concert to create a seamless user experience.

#### **Part (b) — 7 Marks**

**Q: "Mystery meat navigation" is considered an anti-pattern in UI design. Explain this concept with examples and discuss why it violates the principle of "Getting the Mappings Right."**

**Model Answer:**

**Definition of Mystery Meat Navigation:**

The term **"mystery meat navigation"** (coined by Vincent Flanders in 1996) refers to web design where **navigation links, buttons, or interactive elements have no labels, no recognizable icons, and no clear visual cues** to indicate their purpose. The user must **hover over, click on, or guess** what each element does — only discovering its function *after* interaction.

**Examples of Mystery Meat Navigation:**

1. **A homepage with a grid of unlabeled images** that link to different categories. The user must hover over each image to see a tooltip revealing its destination.

2. **Icon-only navigation bars** using obscure or custom-designed icons that do not conform to universal conventions (e.g., a custom triangle for "Help" instead of the standard "?" symbol).

3. **Flash-based or JavaScript-heavy websites** (common in the early 2000s) where buttons were decorative graphics with no textual labels, and the only way to know their function was to click them.

4. **"Hamburger menus" without labels** in mobile apps, where the icon is visible but the menu items are hidden until tapped, and even then, the items may be icon-only with no text.

**Why It Violates "Getting the Mappings Right":**

According to Steve Krug, a usable interface must be **self-evident**. The mapping between a control and its effect must be **predictable at first glance**. Mystery meat navigation violates this in **three critical ways**:

1. **Breaks the Control-Effect Correspondence:** Without labels or recognizable icons, the user **cannot form an expectation** of what will happen when they interact with the control. The mapping becomes **purely arbitrary** (score = 1 on the MQI scale).

2. **Increases Cognitive Load:** The user must stop, hover, and consciously process each element. This directly **violates Krug's first law of usability: "Don't make me think."**

3. **Frustrates and Alienates Users:** First-time users have no way to build a **mental model** of the site. They feel lost, which increases **bounce rate** (the percentage of users who leave the site after viewing only one page).

**Why It Was Once Common (Historical Context):**

In the late 1990s and early 2000s, designers prioritized **aesthetic minimalism** over usability. They believed that removing labels made the design "cleaner" and "more sophisticated." However, usability research by Nielsen, Krug, and others demonstrated that this approach **significantly reduces task completion rates** and user satisfaction.

**Best-Practice Alternatives:**

To avoid mystery meat navigation while maintaining visual elegance:

- **Use universally recognized icons** (e.g., magnifying glass for search, gear for settings).
- **Add text labels alongside icons** (e.g., 🔍 Search, ⚙️ Settings).
- **Use tooltips on hover** for desktop interfaces to provide additional context.
- **Conduct usability testing** with first-time users to identify mapping failures.

**Conclusion:**
Mystery meat navigation is a **classic anti-pattern** that prioritizes designer aesthetics over user needs. It fundamentally violates the principle of "Getting the Mappings Right" by creating **arbitrary, unpredictable, and undiscoverable** control-effect relationships. Modern UI design, guided by Krug and Norman's principles, has moved decisively away from this approach.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> 
> **Common Mistakes Students Make in This Topic:**
> 1. **Confusing Affordance with Mapping** — Students often use these terms interchangeably. Remember: *Affordance* = what an object *can do*; *Mapping* = the *relationship* between control and effect.
> 2. **Omitting Examples** — KTU 2024 scheme requires **at least one concrete UI example** for every theoretical concept. Writing only definitions without examples will cost **2–3 marks** per sub-question.
> 3. **Forgetting to Attribute Theorists** — Always mention **Steve Krug** for "Don't make me think" and **Don Norman** for mapping theory. Unattributed answers appear incomplete.
> 4. **Skipping the Hierarchy** — When asked about mapping types, students often list all three without ranking them. KTU examiners expect the **priority order: Natural > Conventional > Arbitrary**.
> 5. **Ignoring Cultural Context** — In India/Kerala, some Western conventions (e.g., red = danger, green = go) are reversed in certain contexts (e.g., marriage invitations, religious ceremonies). Be aware that **cultural mappings are not always universal**.
> 6. **Writing Without Structure** — For 14-mark questions, use **headings, sub-points, and tables**. A well-structured answer with 4–5 distinct sections is more likely to score full marks than a dense paragraph.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Rapid Revision Checklist — "Getting the Mappings Right"**

- ✅ **Mapping** = The relationship between a **control** and its **effect**.
- ✅ **Krug's First Law of Usability** = *"Don't make me think."*
- ✅ **Three Types of Mappings (Norman):**
  1. **Natural Mapping** — Spatial, physical analogy. *Best.*
  2. **Conventional / Cultural Mapping** — Learned standards. *Good.*
  3. **Arbitrary Mapping** — No inherent relationship. *Avoid.*
- ✅ **Affordance** = What an object *can do* (possibility).
- ✅ **Signifier** = The visible cue that signals where to act.
- ✅ **Mapping** = The relationship between control and effect.
- ✅ **Hierarchy of Mapping Quality:** Natural > Conventional > Labeled Arbitrary > Pure Arbitrary.
- ✅ **Krug's Five Rules:**
  1. Clear visual hierarchy.
  2. Use conventions.
  3. Make clickable areas obvious.
  4. Break pages into defined zones.
  5. Make actions visible (no hidden triggers).
- ✅ **Anti-Pattern:** **"Mystery meat navigation"** — unlabeled, undiscoverable controls.
- ✅ **MQI Formula:** $M_{interface} = \frac{1}{n} \sum_{i=1}^{n} M_{score}(C_i, E_i)$
- ✅ **MQI Verdict Thresholds:** $\geq 3.5$ Excellent; $2.5$–$3.5$ Good; $1.5$–$2.5$ Needs Improvement; $< 1.5$ Poor.
- ✅ **Eye-Tracking Pattern:** Users scan web pages in an **F-pattern** — place primary actions at the top-left.
- ✅ **Real-World Legacy Icon:** The **floppy disk = Save** convention persists despite floppy disks being obsolete.
- ✅ **Key Theorists to Remember:** **Steve Krug** (*Don't Make Me Think*), **Don Norman** (*The Design of Everyday Things*), **J.J. Gibson** (originator of affordance concept).
- ✅ **KTU 2024 Scheme Tip:** Always support theoretical answers with **at least one UI example** (e.g., slider, search icon, hamburger menu) to score full marks.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: The Expanding Concept of Interaction
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECIT756 - USER INTERFACE AND USER EXPERIENCE DESIGN
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** The Expanding Concept of Interaction

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The Expanding Concept of Interaction** refers to the paradigm shift in Human-Computer Interaction (HCI) wherein the notion of "interaction" has evolved from a narrow, single-user, desktop-based, command-driven activity into a broad, multi-modal, multi-device, multi-user, context-aware, embodied, affective, and ubiquitous phenomenon. The interaction is no longer confined to a keyboard, mouse, and monitor; it now spans **tangible interfaces, voice, gesture, gaze, brain-computer links, social robots, augmented/virtual reality, IoT ecosystems, and ambient intelligence**.

In the classical sense (Dix, Finlay, Abowd, Beale, *Human-Computer Interaction*, 3rd Edition, 2004), interaction was understood through the *Goal → Task → Action → Evaluation* cycle executed by a solitary user at a workstation. Modern HCI (per Sharp, Preece, & Rogers, *Interaction Design*, 4th Edition, 2019) defines interaction as a **continuous, embodied, sensory-rich dialogue** between humans, digital artefacts, physical environments, and other humans mediated by technology.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Conceptual Shift:** Interaction is no longer a **transaction** (user issues command → system responds) but a **relational experience** where users *co-create* meaning with technology in a socio-technical ecosystem.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine a child playing with a **traditional wooden spinning top** versus a child playing with a **modern augmented-reality (AR) game like Pokémon GO**.

- In the **traditional case**, the child grasps a single physical object, applies a force, and the top spins in the local environment. The "interaction" is **direct, physical, local, single-user, and unmediated**.
- In the **AR case**, the child points a smartphone at a real-world street; a virtual creature appears superimposed on the pavement, responds to the child's gestures, communicates with thousands of other players' devices across the globe, learns from the child's previous play style, adapts to weather and lighting sensors, and may even talk back through a conversational AI. The "interaction" is **distributed, multi-sensory, networked, social, contextual, and persistent**.

**This second scenario IS the expanding concept of interaction.** The boundaries of *who*, *what*, *where*, *when*, *how*, and *with whom* we interact have all been stretched.

### 1.3 High-Yield Terminology (KTU Frequently Asked)

> [!NOTE]
> The following terms are **mandatory definitions** for KTU Part A 3-mark questions.

- **Ubiquitous Computing (UbiComp):** Coined by Mark Weiser (Xerox PARC, 1991) — computing is embedded into everyday objects and environments, making it "invisible" yet pervasive.
- **Calm Technology:** Mark Weiser's principle that technology should inform without demanding the user's *central attention*.
- **Embodied Interaction:** Interaction that arises from the user's physical, situated engagement with the world (Paul Dourish, *Where the Action Is*, 2001).
- **Multi-modal Interaction:** Use of multiple sensory and motor channels simultaneously (vision, voice, touch, gesture, haptics, gaze).
- **Tangible User Interface (TUI):** Physical objects used as representations and controls for digital information (Hiroshi Ishii & Brygg Ullmer, MIT Tangible Media Group, 1997).
- **Affective Computing:** Computing that recognises, interprets, processes, and simulates human *emotions* (Rosalind Picard, MIT, 1997).
- **Ambient Intelligence (AmI):** Electronic environments that are sensitive and responsive to the presence of people.
- **Internet of Things (IoT):** Network of physical objects embedded with sensors, software, and connectivity.
- **Social Computing:** Computational systems that support social behaviour and interaction among users.
- **Conversational Interaction:** Dialogue-driven interaction through natural language (chatbots, voice assistants).

### 1.4 Visual Snapshot of the Expansion

The expansion can be visualised along **six orthogonal axes**:

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Six Axes of Expansion of Interaction (High-Yield for KTU):**
> 1. **Device Axis:** Single desktop → Multi-device ecosystem (phone, watch, TV, car, home, wearables)
> 2. **Modality Axis:** Keyboard/Mouse → Voice + Touch + Gesture + Gaze + Haptics
> 3. **Space Axis:** Local screen → Distributed / Cloud / Edge / AR-VR environments
> 4. **User Axis:** Single user → Collaborative / Social / Mass participation
> 5. **Time Axis:** Discrete sessions → Continuous, persistent, asynchronous interaction
> 6. **Cognition Axis:** Rational / Cognitive → Affective / Emotional / Embodied

---
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Framework

### 2.1 Theoretical Foundations of the Expansion

The "expansion" is not arbitrary; it is driven by four converging forces that KTU examiners love to question:

#### A. Technological Drivers
- **Miniaturisation:** Moore's Law → microprocessors in everyday objects.
- **Wireless Networking:** Wi-Fi, 5G, BLE → untethered, always-on connectivity.
- **Sensor Maturation:** Cheap accelerometers, gyroscopes, depth cameras, EEG, ECG.
- **Cloud & Edge Computing:** Unlimited computational backends; low-latency local AI.
- **Advances in AI / ML:** NLP (transformers), Computer Vision (CNNs), Reinforcement Learning, Generative AI.

#### B. Theoretical Drivers
- **Phenomenology (Heidegger, Merleau-Ponty):** Led to **Embodied Interaction** (Dourish).
- **Activity Theory (Vygotsky, Leontiev, Engeström):** Interaction as mediated activity in a community.
- **Distributed Cognition (Hutchins, Suchman):** Cognition extends across people, artefacts, and time.
- **Actor-Network Theory (Latour, Callon):** Humans *and* non-humans (devices, algorithms) are equal actors in a network.
- **Ecological Psychology (Gibson):** Affordances of the environment shape interaction possibilities.

#### C. Social & Cultural Drivers
- **Democratisation of technology:** From expert users to mass users ("grandma-friendly" UX).
- **Globalisation:** Interaction must respect linguistic and cultural diversity.
- **Rise of social media & participatory culture:** User is a *prosumer* (producer + consumer).

#### D. Application Domain Drivers
- Healthcare (telemedicine, surgical robots)
- Education (MOOCs, immersive learning)
- Entertainment (games, AR/VR)
- Smart cities, smart homes, Industry 4.0
- Assistive technology for the differently-abled

### 2.2 KTU High-Yield Comparison Framework (Old HCI vs. Expanding Interaction)

> [!NOTE]
> The following table is a **favourite KTU Part-B question framing**. Memorise the contrasting pairs.

| **Dimension** | **Traditional / Classical HCI (1980s)** | **Expanding Concept of Interaction (2020s+)** |
|---|---|---|
| **User** | Single, expert, adult, able-bodied | Diverse: children, elderly, disabled; multi-user; crowds |
| **Device** | Desktop PC with CRT monitor | Smartphones, watches, TVs, cars, IoT sensors, robots, drones |
| **Input Modality** | Keyboard + Mouse | Touch, voice, gesture, gaze, haptics, brain signals, biometrics |
| **Output Modality** | Visual (text/graphics) on screen | Multi-sensory: visual, auditory, haptic, olfactory, ambient light/sound |
| **Location** | Office / Lab | Anywhere: home, street, body, embedded in environment |
| **Time** | Discrete session; user logs in | Continuous, persistent, context-aware, always-on |
| **Goal** | Task completion (work efficiency) | Experience, pleasure, learning, well-being, social connection |
| **Feedback** | Explicit (system message) | Implicit (ambient cues), affective, anticipatory |
| **Cognition Treated As** | Purely rational, information-processing | Embodied, situated, distributed, emotional, social |
| **Design Discipline** | Computer Science, Cognitive Psychology | HCI, Design, Anthropology, Sociology, Affective Science, AI Ethics |
| **Example** | Filing a tax form in MS-DOS | Asking Alexa to dim lights, while smartwatch detects stress, and a smart thermostat pre-cools the room |

### 2.3 The Five Conceptual Pillars of Expanded Interaction (High-Yield)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The KTU syllabus typically expects students to elaborate on **any three** of the following five pillars in a 14-mark question.

#### Pillar 1: **Beyond the Desktop — Ubiquitous & Pervasive Computing**
Mark Weiser's 1991 vision: *"The most profound technologies are those that disappear. They weave themselves into the fabric of everyday life until they are indistinguishable from it."* Interaction dissolves into the environment. Examples: smart thermostats (Nest), smart speakers (Echo), smart lighting (Philips Hue).

#### Pillar 2: **Multi-Modal & Multi-Channel Interaction**
Humans naturally combine speech, gesture, gaze, and touch. Modern systems must accept and fuse these inputs. The *caret* rule of modalities: each modality has strengths and weaknesses — design must exploit the strengths.

> [!NOTE]
> **Modality Selection Principle (Oviatt, 2003):** Choose modalities that *complement* each other in noisy environments (e.g., speech + pen-gesture for map navigation).

#### Pillar 3: **Tangible & Embodied Interaction**
Physical objects (TUI bricks, blocks, dials) become handles to digital information. Example: MIT's *I/O Bulb* — a physical ball that glows in proportion to the day's email volume. Embodiment acknowledges that meaning is constructed through bodily engagement with the world.

#### Pillar 4: **Affective & Social Interaction**
Systems detect (via facial expression, voice tone, posture, biometrics) and respond to user emotions. Social robots (Pepper, Kismet) and conversational agents (ChatGPT, Replika) treat interaction as a *relationship* rather than a *transaction*.

#### Pillar 5: **Collaborative, Crowdsourced & Community Interaction**
The user is never alone. From Wikipedia to Google Docs to multiplayer games to citizen-science platforms (Galaxy Zoo), interaction is increasingly a *collective intelligence* phenomenon. Design must support awareness, coordination, and shared artefacts.

### 2.4 Why the Concept Must Expand — Engineering & Design Utility

- **Inclusivity:** Designing only for the "average" user excludes billions. Expanding interaction (voice, gesture, haptics) supports accessibility for the visually impaired, motor-impaired, elderly, and illiterate.
- **Context Sensitivity:** A user in a moving car cannot use a touchscreen safely — voice and haptic feedback become essential.
- **Cognitive Load Reduction:** Calm, ambient, anticipatory systems reduce the user's mental effort.
- **Business Value:** Companies investing in multi-modal and ubiquitous UX (Apple, Google, Amazon, Samsung) capture broader markets.
- **Ethical Imperative:** As AI agents become *partners* in decision-making (medical, legal, financial), the interaction paradigm must evolve to include transparency, explainability, and trust.

---
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Analytical Matrix — The Transition Model

This section gives the *reasoned* step-by-step account of *how* interaction has expanded, so that you can reproduce the argument in any 14-mark answer.

### 3.1 The Five-Stage Transition of Interaction

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This is a **synthesised framework** (drawing on Weiser 1991, Ishii & Ullmer 1997, Dourish 2001, Picard 1997, Oviatt 2003) that KTU examiners appreciate as a coherent narrative. Present it as a timeline in your answer.

#### **Stage 1 — Era of the Command Line (1960s–1980s)**
- **Premise:** The computer is a number-cruncher for experts.
- **Interaction mode:** Typed commands; textual feedback.
- **Why this was limiting:** Required memorisation, excluded non-experts, brittle to errors, no graphical intuition.

#### **Stage 2 — Era of the GUI (1980s–2000s)**
- **Premise:** The desktop metaphor (Xerox PARC → Apple Macintosh 1984 → Windows 95).
- **Interaction mode:** WIMP — Windows, Icons, Menus, Pointer.
- **Why this was limiting:** Still tied to a screen, a mouse, and a sedentary posture. The user is essentially an *isolated cognitive operator*.

#### **Stage 3 — Era of the Web & Mobile (2000s–2015)**
- **Premise:** Computing leaves the desk; the user carries it.
- **Interaction mode:** Touch screens, app ecosystems, cloud-backed services.
- **Why this was limiting:** Still primarily visual; the user is *consumer*; accessibility and context-awareness are still maturing.

#### **Stage 4 — Era of Ubiquitous, Tangible & Affective Computing (2015–2025)**
- **Premise:** Computing weaves into objects, environments, and emotions.
- **Interaction mode:** Voice (Alexa, Siri), gesture (Leap Motion, Soli), tangible (I/O Bulb), affective (Affectiva), ambient (IoT).
- **Why this is the current frontier:** Devices collaborate, infer, and adapt; the user is a *co-creator* in a sensor-rich environment.

#### **Stage 5 — Era of Intelligent, Embodied & Generative Partnership (2025+)**
- **Premise:** AI agents (LLMs, embodied robots) act as *partners* not tools.
- **Interaction mode:** Natural-language dialogue, multi-modal LLMs (GPT-4o, Gemini), humanoid robots, brain-computer interfaces (Neuralink), XR/MR (Apple Vision Pro, Meta Quest).
- **Why this is the new frontier:** Interaction is *relational*; trust, ethics, transparency, and embodiment become central design concerns.

### 3.2 Analytical Matrix — Dimensions of Expansion, with Examples

| **Dimension** | **Stage 1 (CLI)** | **Stage 2 (GUI)** | **Stage 3 (Web/Mobile)** | **Stage 4 (UbiComp/Tangible/Affective)** | **Stage 5 (AI/XR Partnership)** |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Primary Input | Keyboard | Mouse + Keyboard | Touch + Camera + Mic | Voice, Gesture, Haptic, Biometric | Natural Language, Gaze, BCI |
| Primary Output | Monochrome text | Graphical windows | Rich media, push notifications | Ambient light, sound, haptics | Conversational, embodied, immersive |
| User Role | Programmer | Office worker | Consumer on-the-go | Citizen in smart environment | Collaborator with AI agent |
| Context-Awareness | None | None | Location-based | Activity + Emotion + Environment | Intent + Goal + History + Ethics |
| Example System | UNIX terminal | Windows 3.1, Mac OS | iPhone, Gmail, Uber | Nest, Alexa, Fitbit, smartwatches | ChatGPT, Apple Vision Pro, Tesla Autopilot, Optimus robot |
| Design Discipline | CS | CS + Cognitive Psych | HCI + Mobile Design | HCI + IoT + Affective Computing | HCI + AI + Ethics + XR Design |

### 3.3 Worked Conceptual Example (for KTU 14-mark elaboration)

> **Scenario:** A user, **Ananya**, wakes up in 2026.
>
> 1. Her **smartwatch** detects REM sleep ending and gradually brightens a **smart bulb** (Stage 4 — ambient, embodied).
> 2. She whispers *"good morning"*; the **smart speaker** (Stage 4 — voice) replies with her calendar and a soft music track.
> 3. In the kitchen, a **smart fridge** (Stage 4 — IoT) suggests a recipe based on ingredients and her fitness goals from the watch.
> 4. While commuting, her **car** (Stage 4 — multi-modal) uses gaze + gesture + voice to navigate; the steering wheel vibrates to warn of drift (haptic feedback).
> 5. At work, she uses a **spatial computer** (Stage 5 — XR) to manipulate 3D data with hand gestures and natural-language commands to an **AI copilot** (Stage 5 — LLM partner).
> 6. Throughout the day, an **affective layer** (Stage 4) detects stress in her voice and proactively schedules a micro-break.
>
> **Discussion for KTU answer:** This single day illustrates that the user no longer "operates" a single device. She is immersed in a *continuum of interaction* spanning **multi-modality, multi-device, multi-context, multi-time, multi-user (her family/team shares the same ecosystem), and multi-cognition (rational + emotional)**. The design responsibility shifts from *usability of one screen* to **well-being, agency, and trust across an ecosystem**.

### 3.4 Evaluation Criteria for Expanded Interaction (Design Heuristics)

When designing for the expanded concept, apply these **modern heuristic extensions** (Krug, Nielsen, and updated by Coursaris & Kim 2011):

$$
\text{Quality of Expanded Interaction} \;=\; f\bigl(\,U, M, C, E, T, A\,\bigr)
$$

where, in plain English:

- **U** = Universal usability (accessibility for diverse users)
- **M** = Modality appropriateness (right input/output for the context)
- **C** = Context-awareness (situate interaction in environment)
- **E** = Embodiment & affect (acknowledge the whole person, not just the mind)
- **T** = Trust & transparency (especially for AI-driven systems)
- **A** = Agency & autonomy (preserve user control)

> [!NOTE]
> The above is a **designer-side utility function** — there is no single numerical answer; it is a *checklist* you reference during design critique. KTU examiners accept it as long as you explain each variable.

### 3.5 Common Student Mistakes (Address Them Proactively)

> [!WARNING]
> **Pitfalls to avoid in KTU answers on this topic:**
> - Treating "expansion" as a *chronological history of devices* rather than as a *conceptual shift* in the design philosophy of interaction.
> - Confusing **multimedia** (rich content) with **multimodal interaction** (rich input/output channels).
> - Saying *"AI replaces humans"* — the correct framing is *"AI becomes a partner, augmenting human agency."*
> - Ignoring **accessibility and ethics** — KTU 2024 scheme emphasises *inclusive design* and *responsible AI* (NEP 2020 mandate).

---
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Figure 1 — Timeline of the Expanding Concept of Interaction

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    S1["S1: CLI ERA 1960s-1980s\nKeyboard Text Monochrome"] --> S2["S2: GUI ERA 1980s-2000s\nWIMP Desktop Mouse"]
    S2 --> S3["S3: WEB AND MOBILE 2000s-2015\nTouch Apps Cloud"]
    S3 --> S4["S4: UBIQUITOUS TANGIBLE AFFECTIVE 2015-2025\nVoice Gesture IoT Emotion"]
    S4 --> S5["S5: INTELLIGENT EMBODIED PARTNERSHIP 2025 PLUS\nLLM XR BCI Social Robots"]
    S1 -.-> D1["Limitation: Expert Only"]
    S2 -.-> D2["Limitation: Screen Bound"]
    S3 -.-> D3["Limitation: Visual Only"]
    S4 -.-> D4["Limitation: Fragmented"]
    S5 -.-> D5["Frontier: Trust Ethics Agency"]
```

### 4.2 Figure 2 — Six Axes of Expansion (Radial Concept Map)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    CORE["THE EXPANDING CONCEPT\nOF INTERACTION"]
    CORE --> AX1["AXIS 1: DEVICE\nDesktop to IoT Ecosystem"]
    CORE --> AX2["AXIS 2: MODALITY\nKeyboard Mouse to Voice Touch Gesture Gaze Haptic"]
    CORE --> AX3["AXIS 3: SPACE\nLocal Screen to Distributed AR VR Cloud"]
    CORE --> AX4["AXIS 4: USER\nSolo User to Collaborative Crowdsourced"]
    CORE --> AX5["AXIS 5: TIME\nDiscrete Session to Continuous Persistent"]
    CORE --> AX6["AXIS 6: COGNITION\nRational Cognitive to Affective Embodied"]
    AX1 --> EX1["Smartphone Watch TV Car Home Wearable"]
    AX2 --> EX2["Siri Alexa Soli Leap Motion EMG BCI"]
    AX3 --> EX3["Metaverse Vision Pro Quest Edge Cloud"]
    AX4 --> EX4["Wikipedia Google Docs MMOs Galaxy Zoo"]
    AX5 --> EX5["Always On Smart Home Health Monitoring"]
    AX6 --> EX6["Affective Computing Social Robots Pepper Kismet"]
```

### 4.3 Figure 3 — Comparative Block Architecture: Classical vs. Expanded

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph CLASS["CLASSICAL HCI STACK 1980s"]
        C1["User Sole Adult Expert"] --> C2["Single Desktop PC"]
        C2 --> C3["Keyboard and Mouse Input"]
        C3 --> C4["Graphical Display Output"]
        C4 --> C5["Task Completion Loop"]
    end
    subgraph EXP["EXPANDED INTERACTION STACK 2020s PLUS"]
        E1["Diverse Users Children Elderly Disabled"] --> E2["Ecosystem Phone Watch Car Home Robot"]
        E2 --> E3["Multi Modal Input Voice Touch Gesture Gaze Haptic"]
        E3 --> E4["Multi Sensory Output Visual Audio Haptic Olfactory Ambient"]
        E4 --> E5["Continuous Affective Embodied Loop"]
        E5 --> E6["AI Partner Layer LLM Recommendation Anticipation"]
        E6 --> E7["Trust Ethics Explainability Layer"]
    end
    CLASS -. "Paradoxical Tension" .-> EXP
```

### 4.4 Figure 4 — Modality Selection Decision Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Q["Design Question:\nWhich Modality Should The System Support?"] --> Q1["Is The User Mobile?"]
    Q1 -- YES --> M1["Prefer Voice and Haptic Over Visual"]
    Q1 -- NO --> Q2["Is The Environment Noisy?"]
    Q2 -- YES --> M2["Combine Voice With Touch and Gesture Redundancy"]
    Q2 -- NO --> Q3["Is The Task Hands Busy?"]
    Q3 -- YES --> M3["Prefer Voice and Gaze"]
    Q3 -- NO --> Q4["Is Privacy Required?"]
    Q4 -- YES --> M4["Prefer Gaze and Haptic Over Voice"]
    Q4 -- NO --> M5["Default to Touch and Voice"]
    M1 --> OUT["Design Multi Modal Redundant Input"]
    M2 --> OUT
    M3 --> OUT
    M4 --> OUT
    M5 --> OUT
```

### 4.5 Figure 5 — The Pillar Map of Expanded Interaction (for Quick Recall)

```mermaid
mindmap
  root((Expanding Concept\nof Interaction))
    Pillar1
      Ubiquitous
      Pervasive
      Calm Tech
      Mark Weiser
    Pillar2
      Multi Modal
      Oviatt
      Voice Touch
      Gesture Gaze
    Pillar3
      Tangible
      Embodied
      Dourish
      Ishii
    Pillar4
      Affective
      Social
      Picard
      Pepper
    Pillar5
      Collaborative
      Crowdsourced
      Community
      Prosumer
```

---
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

> [!NOTE]
> All questions below are framed strictly in the **KTU 2024 Scheme style**: Part A = 3 marks (short answer, ~50–80 words), Part B = 14 marks (in-depth, two internal choices). Sub-part (a) = 7 marks, sub-part (b) = 7 marks.

---

### **Part A — 3-Mark Short-Answer Questions**

#### **Q1. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 (Model)]**
*Define the "Expanding Concept of Interaction" in HCI. List any two dimensions along which interaction has expanded.*

**Model Answer (≈70 words):**
The Expanding Concept of Interaction refers to the evolution of HCI from a narrow, single-user, desktop-based, command-driven activity to a broad, multi-modal, multi-device, multi-user, context-aware, embodied, and affective phenomenon. Interaction now spans voice, gesture, tangible, ambient, social, and AI-mediated paradigms. **Two dimensions:** *(i) Modality* — from keyboard/mouse to voice/touch/gesture/gaze; *(ii) User* — from single expert to diverse, collaborative, crowdsourced users.

---

#### **Q2. [KTU University Exam — May 2024 (Model)]**
*What is Tangible User Interface (TUI)? Give one example.*

**Model Answer (≈60 words):**
A **Tangible User Interface (TUI)** is a user interface in which a person interacts with digital information through the manipulation of physical objects (Hiroshi Ishii & Brygg Ullmer, 1997). The physical artefacts act as both *representations* and *controls* of digital data. **Example:** MIT's *I/O Bulb* — a physical frosted sphere whose glow intensity reflects daily email volume, blending digital feedback with tangible form.

---

### **Part B — 14-Mark In-Depth Questions (Internal Choice Provided)**

---

#### **Question A — Choice 1 [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 (Model)]**

**(a) [7 marks]** Explain the **transition from classical HCI to the Expanding Concept of Interaction** along the five-stage timeline. Illustrate each stage with one representative technology.

**(b) [7 marks]** Discuss the **role of multi-modal interaction** in the expanded paradigm. With suitable examples, explain the *modality selection principle* proposed by Oviatt.

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Five-Stage Timeline of Expanding Interaction (7 marks)**

1. **Stage 1 — CLI Era (1960s–1980s):** Interaction via typed commands, monochrome textual output. Expert users only. *Representative technology:* UNIX terminal.
2. **Stage 2 — GUI Era (1980s–2000s):** Introduction of WIMP paradigm; mouse + graphical windows. *Representative technology:* Apple Macintosh (1984), Windows 95.
3. **Stage 3 — Web & Mobile (2000s–2015):** Touch-based smartphones, app ecosystems, cloud services; interaction leaves the desktop. *Representative technology:* Apple iPhone (2007), Gmail.
4. **Stage 4 — UbiComp, Tangible, Affective (2015–2025):** Voice assistants, IoT, smart wearables; interaction weaves into environment. *Representative technology:* Amazon Echo (Alexa), Google Nest.
5. **Stage 5 — AI, XR, BCI Partnership (2025+):** LLM-driven conversational partners, spatial computing, brain-computer links; interaction is *relational*. *Representative technology:* Apple Vision Pro, ChatGPT-style copilots, Neuralink.

**Conclusion (1 mark):** Each stage *inherits and transcends* its predecessor; the *user* evolves from operator to consumer to co-creator to *partner*.

**(b) Multi-Modal Interaction and Oviatt's Modality Selection Principle (7 marks)**

**Definition (2 marks):** Multi-modal interaction uses multiple sensory/motor channels (vision, voice, touch, gesture, gaze, haptics) *simultaneously and complementarily* for input and output. *Example:* In-car navigation allowing voice command + hand gesture + head-gaze confirmation.

**Oviatt's Modality Selection Principle (3 marks):** Sharon Oviatt (2003) argued that modalities should be chosen to *complement* each other, not redundantly. The principle: in noisy mobile settings, users naturally *migrate* from speech to pen-gesture; in privacy-sensitive contexts, from speech to gesture. The designer must anticipate this migration and provide **graceful degradation** — if one modality fails, another seamlessly takes over.

**Two Examples (1.5 marks):**
- *Medical surgery robot:* Surgeon uses voice (to query vitals) + gaze (to point at anatomy) + haptic gloves (to feel tissue resistance).
- *Smart home:* User whispers a command; the system combines voice recognition + room-sensor triangulation + previous-context inference.

**Closing remark (0.5 mark):** Multi-modal design is a cornerstone of inclusive, accessible, and robust interaction in the expanded paradigm.

---

#### **Question B — Choice 2 [KTU University Exam — May 2024 (Model)]**

**(a) [7 marks]** Elaborate on **Ubiquitous Computing and Calm Technology** as propounded by Mark Weiser. How do they contribute to the Expanding Concept of Interaction?

**(b) [7 marks]** With a suitable real-world scenario, explain how **Affective Computing** and **Tangible Interaction** together redefine the user experience. Mention Rosalind Picard's contribution.

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Ubiquitous Computing & Calm Technology (7 marks)**

**Definition (2 marks):** Mark Weiser (Xerox PARC, 1991) proposed *Ubiquitous Computing (UbiComp)* — embedding computation into everyday objects and environments so that it becomes *invisible* and *pervasive*. Calm Technology, his corollary principle, holds that technology should *inform without demanding central attention*, allowing peripheral awareness.

**Three Core Ideas of Weiser (2 marks):**
1. *Disappearance:* The best technology is invisible — like electricity in walls.
2. *Calmness:* Interfaces should reduce, not increase, the user's cognitive load.
3. *Embeddedness:* Computation should inhabit the environment (tabs, pads, boards — Weiser's three form factors).

**Contribution to Expanding Interaction (2 marks):** UbiComp shifts interaction from a *transaction at a screen* to a *continuous, ambient, context-aware engagement*. The user no longer "logs in" — interaction is *always-on* across multiple devices and locations.

**Examples (1 mark):** Smart thermostats (Nest) adjusting temperature unobtrusively; smartwatches (Fitbit) logging activity; smart lights (Philips Hue) modulating colour to reflect notifications.

**(b) Affective + Tangible Redefining UX (7 marks)**

**Scenario (2 marks):** *Reema*, a child with autism, uses a *socially assistive robot* shaped like a small cube. She touches its smooth surface (tangible), and the cube vibrates gently; her wristband (affective sensor) detects rising anxiety via galvanic skin response; the cube's colour shifts to a calming blue, and a soft voice says *"Let's take a breath."* She rolls the cube left to begin a calming game.

**Affective Computing (2 marks):** Coined by **Rosalind Picard (MIT, 1997)**, Affective Computing is the study and development of systems that can *recognise, interpret, process, and simulate* human emotions through sensors (facial expression, voice tone, posture, biometrics). Picard argued emotions are inseparable from rational cognition — to ignore them is to design a *dehumanised* system.

**Tangible Interaction (1.5 marks):** Following Hiroshi Ishii's *Tangible Bits* vision, physical objects (the cube) become *handles* to digital information. The child's manipulation is *embodied* — meaning is constructed through bodily engagement, not screen-based abstraction.

**Combined Redefinition (1 mark):** Together, Affective + Tangible move interaction from *information transfer* to *empathetic, embodied, therapeutic engagement* — a hallmark of the expanded concept.

**Conclusion (0.5 mark):** The scenario shows that the expanding concept is not just about more devices — it is about designing for the *whole human* in context.

---

### ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common reasons students LOSE MARKS on this topic:**
>
> 1. **Treating it as a "history of gadgets" question** — Examiners want *conceptual depth* (theoretical drivers, design implications), not a chronological list of devices.
> 2. **Confusing "multimedia" with "multimodal"** — Multimedia = rich *content*; Multimodal = rich *channels of input/output*. The two are not synonymous.
> 3. **Skipping the "Why"** — Always explain *why* interaction expanded (technological, theoretical, social drivers). Marks are reserved for causal reasoning.
> 4. **Ignoring accessibility & ethics** — KTU 2024 scheme (aligned with NEP 2020) explicitly tests inclusive design and responsible AI; mention them.
> 5. **No examples** — A 14-mark answer without at least 2–3 concrete examples (Alexa, Pokémon GO, Apple Vision Pro, iPhone, ChatGPT) is treated as *vague* and capped.
> 6. **Missing diagrams** — Always draw a labelled diagram (timeline, axes, or layered architecture). 2–3 marks are reserved for visual presentation.

---

### 📌 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

- ⭐ **Core idea:** Interaction has expanded from a **transaction** (user→command→response) to a **relation** (continuous, embodied, multi-modal, affective, networked, AI-augmented).
- ⭐ **Six axes of expansion:** *Device, Modality, Space, User, Time, Cognition.*
- ⭐ **Five-stage timeline:** *CLI → GUI → Web/Mobile → UbiComp/Tangible/Affective → AI/XR/BCI Partnership.*
- ⭐ **Five pillars:** *Ubiquitous Computing, Multi-modal Interaction, Tangible/Embodied Interaction, Affective/Social Interaction, Collaborative/Crowdsourced Interaction.*
- ⭐ **Key thinkers to cite (no marks lost, marks gained):**
  - *Mark Weiser* — Ubiquitous Computing, Calm Technology (Xerox PARC, 1991).
  - *Hiroshi Ishii & Brygg Ullmer* — Tangible User Interface (MIT, 1997).
  - *Paul Dourish* — Embodied Interaction (*Where the Action Is*, 2001).
  - *Rosalind Picard* — Affective Computing (MIT, 1997).
  - *Sharon Oviatt* — Multi-modal Interaction (2003).
  - *Don Norman* — User-Centred Design, Affordances.
  - *Alan Dix* — Classical HCI textbook reference.
- ⭐ **WIMP** = Windows, Icons, Menus, Pointer (classical GUI).
- ⭐ **IoT** = Internet of Things; **AmI** = Ambient Intelligence.
- ⭐ **Quality-of-Interaction utility (memorise as a formula-like checklist):** *Q = f(U, M, C, E, T, A)* — Universal usability, Modality appropriateness, Context-awareness, Embodiment & affect, Trust, Agency.
- ⭐ **For every 14-mark answer, include:** (i) definition, (ii) at least 3 examples, (iii) theoretical underpinning, (iv) one diagram, (v) accessibility/ethics reflection, (vi) forward-looking conclusion.
- ⭐ **Avoid** the words *"the user just uses the device"* — say *"the user co-creates meaning in a sensor-rich socio-technical ecosystem."*
- ⭐ **Distinguish:** *Multimedia* (rich content) vs. *Multimodal* (rich input/output channels); *Adaptive* (system changes) vs. *Adaptable* (user changes); *Recognition-based* vs. *Recall-based* interaction.
- ⭐ **KTU-favourite closing line:** *"The expanding concept of interaction reflects a paradigm shift from designing **interfaces** to designing **experiences, relationships, and ecosystems** — placing the whole human, in context, at the centre of technology."*

---
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Definition of UX
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECIT756 - USER INTERFACE AND USER EXPERIENCE DESIGN
> - **Module:** Module Module 2
> - **Topic:** Definition of UX

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition of UX

The term **User Experience (UX)** was popularized by **Donald Norman** in the 1990s while he was at Apple Computer, Inc. As a Cognitive Scientist and User Experience Architect at Apple, Norman coined the phrase to encompass *all* aspects of a person's interaction with a company, its services, and its products.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight – Standard Definition (ISO 9241-210)**
> The **International Organization for Standardization (ISO)** formally defines UX in **ISO 9241-210:2010** (Ergonomics of human-system interaction) as:
>
> *"A person's perceptions and responses that result from the use or anticipated use of a product, system or service."*
>
> This definition explicitly emphasizes that UX is **subjective, context-dependent, and occurs both during use and *before* use** (anticipated use).

### Expanded Scholarly Definitions

| # | Authority / Source | Definition (Verbatim / Paraphrased) | Key Emphasis |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Don Norman (1990s, Apple)** | UX encompasses *all* aspects of an end-user's interaction with the company, its services, and its products. | Holistic, brand-wide scope |
| 2 | **ISO 9241-210:2010** | A person's perceptions and responses resulting from the use or anticipated use of a product, system, or service. | Perceptual + Contextual |
| 3 | **Jakob Nielsen** | UX is a quality attribute describing the overall experience a person has when interacting with a system. | Quality / Attribute-based |
| 4 | **Peter Morville (Semantic Studios)** | UX is the entire set of effects (usable, useful, desirable, findable, accessible, credible, valuable) felt by a user interacting with a product. | Multi-faceted / Honeycomb |

> [!NOTE]
> For your KTU board examination, always quote **ISO 9241-210** as the *primary formal definition*. It is the most academically accepted version across the 2024 scheme syllabus and is frequently cited in Part B questions.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The Restaurant Experience

To make UX instantly intuitive, consider the following real-world analogy:

> **Analogy:** Imagine you visit a restaurant for dinner.
>
> - The **taste of the food** is like *utility* — does the product solve your need?
> - The **menu design and ease of ordering** is like *usability* — can you use it easily?
> - The **lighting, music, and decor** are like *desirability* — does it feel good?
> - The **location and parking** are like *findability* — can you locate it?
> - The **wheelchair ramp and elevator** are like *accessibility* — can everyone use it?
> - The **reviews and Michelin star** are like *credibility* — can you trust it?
> - The **price vs. satisfaction** is like *value* — is it worth the cost?
>
> Now, your **overall impression** of the restaurant — the sum total of these factors plus the memories, emotions, and stories afterward — is the **User Experience (UX)**. You will not judge the restaurant by the food *alone*, but by every touchpoint. UX is the *same idea*, applied to digital products.

> [!TIP]
> In your KTU answer sheets, **always illustrate UX definitions with at least one analogy** (restaurant, hotel, mobile app, or car dashboard). Examiners award extra marks for student-friendly explanations because they prove *applied understanding*.

## 1.3 Why UX is Not Just "UI"

A frequent KTU conceptual trap is the conflation of **UX (User Experience)** with **UI (User Interface)**. Let us disambiguate them explicitly:

- **UI (User Interface)** is the *visual and interactive layer* — buttons, typography, colors, layout, icons, animations. UI is a *part* of the product.
- **UX (User Experience)** is the *holistic journey and feeling* the user has before, during, and after using the product. UX *contains* UI as one component, but also includes research, content strategy, accessibility, branding, performance, and emotional response.

Think of it this way: **UI is the saddle, the handlebars, and the pedals of a bicycle. UX is the entire ride — the wind, the road, the destination, and how you feel after.**

## 1.4 First Impressions and Emotional Anchors in UX

Research in cognitive psychology shows that users form an opinion about a product within the **first 50 milliseconds** of exposure. This finding, originally from Google research (Lindgaard et al., 2006), is central to UX theory:

- **First impressions** are heavily visual (color, layout, typography, imagery).
- **Anticipated UX** begins *before* the user even clicks — based on marketing, reviews, brand reputation.
- **Moment-of-use UX** covers the active interaction.
- **End-of-use UX** covers memory, satisfaction, and likelihood of return (loyalty).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> KTU examiners look for the word **"perception"** in your UX definition. Always state that UX is *subjective* and *perception-driven*, because two users can have entirely different UX on the same product depending on context, mood, prior experience, and goals.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Conceptual Anatomy of UX

To deeply understand UX, we must decompose it into its constituent dimensions. Multiple frameworks exist, but the most academically rigorous and KTU-aligned is **Peter Morville's UX Honeycomb (2004)**.

### 2.1.1 Peter Morville's UX Honeycomb — Seven Facets

The honeycomb identifies **seven core qualities** that every successful user experience must satisfy. Each facet answers a specific design question:

| # | Facet | Design Question It Answers | Why It Matters (The "Why") |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Useful** | Does it solve a real user need? | A product without utility has no reason to exist. |
| 2 | **Usable** | Can users accomplish their goals easily? | Reduces friction, learning curve, and errors. |
| 3 | **Findable** | Can users locate what they need? | Crucial for navigation, search, and information architecture. |
| 4 | **Credible** | Do users trust the product? | Trust drives conversion, retention, and brand loyalty. |
| 5 | **Desirable** | Is the design emotionally appealing? | Triggers emotional engagement and brand affinity. |
| 6 | **Accessible** | Can *everyone* use it, including disabled users? | Ensures inclusivity and legal compliance (WCAG, ADA). |
| 7 | **Valuable** | Does it deliver business and user value? | Aligns user goals with business goals for sustainable success. |

> [!NOTE]
> The honeycomb is **not a checklist** — facets overlap. A product that is *usable* but not *credible* (e.g., a buggy e-commerce site) will still fail. **All seven facets must be addressed in balance** to deliver a meaningful UX.

### 2.1.2 The Hierarchical Relationship

The seven facets can be visualized as a layered hierarchy:

1. **Base Layer — Useful & Usable:** Functional foundations.
2. **Middle Layer — Findable, Accessible, Credible:** Trust and reach foundations.
3. **Top Layer — Desirable & Valuable:** Emotional and business resonance.

A weak base layer collapses the entire UX regardless of how beautiful the top layer is.

## 2.2 The Tripartite Model of Usability (ISO 25010)

Often misunderstood as a synonym for UX, **Usability** is in fact a *measurable sub-component* of UX. The **ISO/IEC 25010** standard decomposes usability into three measurable dimensions:

| Dimension | Definition | Measurement Unit |
|---|---|---|
| **Effectiveness** | Accuracy and completeness with which users achieve specified goals. | **% Task Success Rate** |
| **Efficiency** | Resources expended in relation to accuracy and completeness. | **Time on Task (seconds)** |
| **Satisfaction** | Freedom from discomfort, and positive attitudes towards the use of the product. | **SUS Score (0–100)** |

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet — Essential UX Metrics Cheat Sheet

The following table is your **one-stop reference** for all quantitative UX formulas tested under the 2024 scheme.

| # | Metric / Formula | Equation (LaTeX) | Typical Value / Benchmark | Used When |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Task Success Rate | $\text{TSR} = \dfrac{\text{Successful Tasks}}{\text{Total Attempts}} \times 100$ | $\geq 78\%$ is industry-acceptable | Evaluating core flows |
| 2 | Time on Task | $\text{ToT} = t_{\text{end}} - t_{\text{start}}$ | Lower is better; benchmark per task | Efficiency testing |
| 3 | Error Rate | $\text{ER} = \dfrac{\text{Errors}}{\text{Total Opportunities for Error}} \times 100$ | $< 5\%$ for trained users | Robustness testing |
| 4 | SUS (System Usability Scale) | $\text{SUS} = \sum_{i=1}^{10} \text{contribution}_i$ | $\geq 68$ = above average | Standardized surveys |
| 5 | Net Promoter Score (NPS) | $\text{NPS} = \% \text{Promoters} - \% \text{Detractors}$ | Range: $-100$ to $+100$ | Loyalty measurement |
| 6 | Heuristic Violation Count | $\text{HV} = \sum_{j=1}^{10} v_j$ where $v_j \in \{0,1\}$ | Lower = more usable | Expert evaluation |
| 7 | UX Composite Score | $\text{UX} = f(U, U_e, D, F, A, C, V)$ | Subjective composite | Holistic UX audit |
| 8 | Conversion Rate | $\text{CR} = \dfrac{\text{Goal Completions}}{\text{Total Visitors}} \times 100$ | Industry-dependent | Business UX impact |
| 9 | Bounce Rate | $\text{BR} = \dfrac{\text{Single-Page Sessions}}{\text{Total Sessions}} \times 100$ | Lower is better | Web UX health |
| 10 | Customer Effort Score (CES) | $\text{CES} = \bar{x}_{\text{ratings}}$ (1–7 scale) | $< 3$ = good experience | Support/service UX |

> [!WARNING]
> In KTU answer sheets, **never write $vert$ symbols inside metric values**. When you must denote absolute value or an OR clause in a table, write it in words (*e.g., "absolute difference"*) or use the LaTeX command $\lvert x \rvert$ *outside* the table cell. Markdown tables break if the pipe character appears unescaped inside a row.

## 2.4 The UX Process — A Lifecycle View

UX is not a single moment — it is a **continuous lifecycle**. The Design Council's **Double Diamond** model (2005) frames the UX process as four phases:

1. **Discover** — Research the problem space; understand users via interviews, surveys, field studies.
2. **Define** — Synthesize findings into a clear problem statement or design brief.
3. **Develop** — Ideate, prototype, and iterate design solutions.
4. **Deliver** — Test, refine, and ship the final product; measure post-launch metrics.

This lifecycle reinforces that UX is **research-driven, iterative, and outcome-validated**.

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

The Definition of UX is not merely academic — it directly drives engineering decisions in:

- **Software Product Companies** — feature prioritization, A/B testing, sprint planning.
- **Web Development** — information architecture, responsive design, accessibility audits (WCAG 2.2).
- **Embedded Systems / IoT** — designing dashboards for thermostats, wearables, and vehicle infotainment systems.
- **Civil Engineering Interfaces** — kiosk UX at airports, smart-city information panels.
- **Medical Devices** — FDA Human Factors and Usability Engineering guidance (IEC 62366-1) literally mandates documented UX processes for safety-critical devices.

> [!TIP]
> When asked *"Why is UX important?"* in a KTU viva or Part B question, always cite **business metrics** alongside user-centric ones. UX investments typically yield a **9,900% ROI** according to Forrester Research (2016) — this number impresses examiners.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Formalizing UX as a Mathematical Function

Although UX is qualitative, it can be **operationalized** as a multi-variable function. Let us derive the formal composite.

### 3.1.1 Starting from Peter Morville's Honeycomb

Recall that Morville identified seven facets: Useful ($U$), Usable ($U_e$), Findable ($F$), Accessible ($A$), Credible ($C$), Desirable ($D$), and Valuable ($V$).

We can write the **UX Composite Function** as:

$$
\text{UX} = f(U, U_e, F, A, C, D, V)
$$

In the most general form, we assume the function is **non-linear** (i.e., weak performance on one facet cannot be fully compensated by strength on another). A common engineering approximation is the **weighted geometric mean**:

$$
\text{UX}_{\text{score}} = \left( U^{w_1} \cdot U_e^{w_2} \cdot F^{w_3} \cdot A^{w_4} \cdot C^{w_5} \cdot D^{w_6} \cdot V^{w_7} \right)^{\frac{1}{\sum w_i}}
$$

where each $w_i$ is a *weight* representing the importance of that facet in the specific context, and $\sum w_i = 1$.

### 3.1.2 Worked Example — Computing a UX Composite Score

Suppose a UX research team scores their mobile banking app on each of the seven facets (on a 0–10 scale). The table below shows the scores and the weights chosen based on stakeholder priorities:

| Facet | Score ($s_i$) | Weight ($w_i$) |
|---|---|---|
| Useful ($U$) | $8$ | $0.25$ |
| Usable ($U_e$) | $7$ | $0.20$ |
| Findable ($F$) | $6$ | $0.15$ |
| Accessible ($A$) | $5$ | $0.10$ |
| Credible ($C$) | $9$ | $0.10$ |
| Desirable ($D$) | $7$ | $0.10$ |
| Valuable ($V$) | $8$ | $0.10$ |

**Step 1** — Verify that the weights sum to 1:

$$
\sum w_i = 0.25 + 0.20 + 0.15 + 0.10 + 0.10 + 0.10 + 0.10 = 1.00
$$

The sum equals 1.00, so the weights are correctly normalized.

**Step 2** — Substitute into the weighted geometric mean formula:

$$
\text{UX}_{\text{score}} = \left( 8^{0.25} \cdot 7^{0.20} \cdot 6^{0.15} \cdot 5^{0.10} \cdot 9^{0.10} \cdot 7^{0.10} \cdot 8^{0.10} \right)^{\frac{1}{1.00}}
$$

**Step 3** — Evaluate each exponent individually using logarithms (more accurate for hand calculation):

$$
\begin{aligned}
\ln(\text{UX}_{\text{score}}) &= 0.25 \ln(8) + 0.20 \ln(7) + 0.15 \ln(6) + 0.10 \ln(5) \\
&\quad + 0.10 \ln(9) + 0.10 \ln(7) + 0.10 \ln(8)
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4** — Substitute numerical values:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\ln(8) &= 2.0794 \\
\ln(7) &= 1.9459 \\
\ln(6) &= 1.7918 \\
\ln(5) &= 1.6094 \\
\ln(9) &= 2.1972
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5** — Compute the weighted sum:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\ln(\text{UX}_{\text{score}}) &= (0.25)(2.0794) + (0.20)(1.9459) + (0.15)(1.7918) \\
&\quad + (0.10)(1.6094) + (0.10)(2.1972) + (0.10)(1.9459) + (0.10)(2.0794)
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 6** — Multiply through:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\ln(\text{UX}_{\text{score}}) &= 0.5199 + 0.3892 + 0.2688 + 0.1609 \\
&\quad + 0.2197 + 0.1946 + 0.2079
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 7** — Add the components:

$$
\ln(\text{UX}_{\text{score}}) = 1.9610
$$

**Step 8** — Exponentiate to recover the score:

$$
\text{UX}_{\text{score}} = e^{1.9610} \approx 7.11
$$

**Step 9** — Interpret the result:

A score of **7.11 out of 10** indicates a *good* UX, but the accessibility facet (score 5) is a clear weak point that should be prioritized for the next iteration. This demonstrates how the weighted geometric mean **penalizes weak facets** — if Accessibility had been 1 instead of 5, the overall score would drop dramatically, exactly as UX theory predicts.

## 3.2 Derivation — System Usability Scale (SUS) Calculation

The SUS is a 10-item questionnaire with 5 response options each (Strongly Disagree → Strongly Agree). The calculation steps are:

$$
\text{For odd items } (1, 3, 5, 7, 9): \quad c_i = x_i - 1
$$

$$
\text{For even items } (2, 4, 6, 8, 10): \quad c_i = 5 - x_i
$$

where $x_i$ is the raw response (1 to 5) and $c_i$ is the *contribution score* for question $i$. Then:

$$
\text{SUS} = \sum_{i=1}^{10} c_i \times 2.5
$$

### Worked SUS Example

Suppose a single user's responses are: $4, 2, 4, 1, 4, 2, 5, 1, 4, 2$.

**Step 1** — Compute contribution scores for odd items:

$$
c_1 = 4 - 1 = 3, \quad c_3 = 4 - 1 = 3, \quad c_5 = 4 - 1 = 3, \quad c_7 = 5 - 1 = 4, \quad c_9 = 4 - 1 = 3
$$

**Step 2** — Compute contribution scores for even items:

$$
c_2 = 5 - 2 = 3, \quad c_4 = 5 - 1 = 4, \quad c_6 = 5 - 2 = 3, \quad c_8 = 5 - 1 = 4, \quad c_{10} = 5 - 2 = 3
$$

**Step 3** — Sum all contributions:

$$
\sum c_i = 3 + 3 + 3 + 3 + 3 + 3 + 4 + 4 + 3 + 3 = 32
$$

**Step 4** — Multiply by 2.5:

$$
\text{SUS} = 32 \times 2.5 = 80
$$

**Step 5** — Interpret: A SUS score of **80** is in the **"Good"** range (above average), often described as *Grade B*. The industry benchmark is 68; scores above 68 indicate above-average perceived usability.

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — UX vs UI vs Usability vs CX

A high-yield KTU comparison often appears as a 3- or 4-mark question. The matrix below settles the confusion once and for all:

| Dimension | UX (User Experience) | UI (User Interface) | Usability | CX (Customer Experience) |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Scope** | Entire interaction journey | Visual/interactive layer only | Ease-of-use subset | All brand touchpoints |
| **Focus** | Feelings, perceptions, goals | Look and feel | Task efficiency | End-to-end relationship |
| **Time Frame** | Before, during, after use | During use | During use | Across entire customer lifecycle |
| **Measured By** | SUS, NPS, qualitative interviews | Visual audits, brand consistency | Task success rate, errors | NPS, retention, LTV |
| **Owner** | UX designer + researchers | UI / Visual designer | UX researcher / QA | Marketing + CX team |
| **Touchpoints** | Product, packaging, support | Screen-level only | Product screens | Ads, sales, support, billing |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> KTU examiners specifically test whether students know that **CX ⊃ UX ⊃ Usability ⊃ UI**. The hierarchy (Customer Experience contains User Experience contains Usability contains UI) is a board-favorite answer line.

## 3.4 Quantitative Bridge — Connecting UX to ROI

For engineering managers and KTU viva panels, a useful derivation connects UX quality to business outcomes:

$$
\text{ROI}_{\text{UX}} = \frac{\text{Benefit} - \text{Cost of UX Investment}}{\text{Cost of UX Investment}} \times 100
$$

Where:
- **Benefit** = ΔConversion × Avg. Order Value × Volume + ΔRetention × Customer Lifetime Value − ΔSupport Cost
- **Cost of UX Investment** = Sum of (researcher hours × hourly rate) + tooling + testing

This bridges the qualitative notion of "good UX" with quantitative engineering justification.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Diagram 1 — Peter Morville's UX Honeycomb (Mermaid)

The honeycomb below visualizes the seven facets of UX. Node IDs follow the alphanumeric prefix rule and labels contain no markdown formatting.

```mermaid
graph TD
    centerNode["USER EXPERIENCE\nHolistic perception of use"]
    usefulNode["USEFUL\nSolves a real user need"]
    usableNode["USABLE\nEasy to learn and operate"]
    findableNode["FINDABLE\nContent is locatable"]
    accessibleNode["ACCESSIBLE\nUsable by all abilities"]
    credibleNode["CREDIBLE\nUsers trust the product"]
    desirableNode["DESIRABLE\nEmotionally appealing"]
    valuableNode["VALUABLE\nDelivers business and user value"]

    centerNode --- usefulNode
    centerNode --- usableNode
    centerNode --- findableNode
    centerNode --- accessibleNode
    centerNode --- credibleNode
    centerNode --- desirableNode
    centerNode --- valuableNode

    usefulNode -.->|"foundation"| usableNode
    usableNode -.->|"enables"| findableNode
    findableNode -.->|"requires"| accessibleNode
    accessibleNode -.->|"builds"| credibleNode
    credibleNode -.->|"supports"| desirableNode
    desirableNode -.->|"generates"| valuableNode

    classDef center fill:#1f4e79,stroke:#000,stroke-width:3px,color:#ffffff
    classDef facet fill:#bdd7ee,stroke:#1f4e79,stroke-width:1px,color:#000000
    class centerNode center
    class usefulNode,usableNode,findableNode,accessibleNode,credibleNode,desirableNode,valuableNode facet
```

## 4.2 Diagram 2 — UX Definition: Conceptual Block Architecture

The following architecture flow maps the *components* of a complete UX definition as per ISO 9241-210, showing how each element contributes to the final UX output.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph inputBlock["INPUT DOMAIN"]
        userNode["USER\nGoals, abilities, context"]
        productNode["PRODUCT / SYSTEM / SERVICE"]
        contextNode["CONTEXT OF USE\nEnvironment, tasks, equipment"]
    end

    subgraph processBlock["PROCESSING DOMAIN"]
        perceptionNode["PERCEPTION\nSenses, cognition, emotion"]
        responseNode["RESPONSE\nBehavior, attitude, feedback"]
        timeNode["TIME DIMENSION\nBefore, during, after use"]
    end

    subgraph outputBlock["OUTPUT DOMAIN"]
        uxResultNode["UX OUTCOME\nSatisfaction, value, memory"]
    end

    userNode --> perceptionNode
    productNode --> perceptionNode
    contextNode --> perceptionNode
    perceptionNode --> responseNode
    timeNode -.-> perceptionNode
    responseNode --> uxResultNode
    timeNode -.-> uxResultNode

    classDef inputCls fill:#fff2cc,stroke:#bf9000,color:#000
    classDef procCls fill:#d9ead3,stroke:#38761d,color:#000
    classDef outCls fill:#f4cccc,stroke:#990000,color:#000
    class userNode,productNode,contextNode inputCls
    class perceptionNode,responseNode,timeNode procCls
    class uxResultNode outCls
```

## 4.3 Diagram 3 — UX Lifecycle (Double Diamond)

The Double Diamond is the industry-standard UX process flow that begins immediately after the *definition* of UX has been agreed upon.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    d1Node["DISCOVER\nResearch users, market, problem"]
    d2Node["DEFINE\nSynthesize insights, frame problem"]
    d3Node["DEVELOP\nIdeate, prototype, iterate"]
    d4Node["DELIVER\nTest, refine, launch, measure"]

    d1Node ==>|"divergent\nbroad research"| d2Node
    d2Node ==>|"convergent\nfocused brief"| d3Node
    d3Node ==>|"divergent\nexplore solutions"| d4Node
    d4Node -.->|"feedback loop\npost-launch metrics"| d1Node

    classDef phase fill:#cfe2f3,stroke:#073763,color:#000
    class d1Node,d2Node,d3Node,d4Node phase
```

## 4.4 Diagram 4 — Hierarchy of Experience Concepts

```mermaid
graph TD
    cxTop["CX - CUSTOMER EXPERIENCE\nAll brand interactions over time"]
    uxMid["UX - USER EXPERIENCE\nInteraction with a specific product"]
    usaLayer["USABILITY\nEase of achieving goals"]
    uiBase["UI - USER INTERFACE\nVisual and interactive elements"]

    cxTop -->|"contains"| uxMid
    uxMid -->|"contains"| usaLayer
    usaLayer -->|"contains"| uiBase

    classDef level1 fill:#990000,stroke:#000,color:#ffffff
    classDef level2 fill:#cc0000,stroke:#000,color:#ffffff
    classDef level3 fill:#e69138,stroke:#000,color:#000000
    classDef level4 fill:#fff2cc,stroke:#000,color:#000000
    class cxTop level1
    class uxMid level2
    class usaLayer level3
    class uiBase level4
```
