- ✅ **Credible** = inspires trust → validated by trust signals (SSL, reviews, badges).
- ✅ **Valuable** = delivers mutual value to user and business → validated by **KPIs (LTV, CAC, NPS)**; LTV/CAC ratio target $\geq 3:1$.
- ✅ **Color contrast ratio formula:** $\frac{L_1 + 0.05}{L_2 + 0.05}$, with a range of $1$ to $21$; minimum required is $4.5:1$ for normal text.
- ✅ **Inter-relationship:** All seven components are **inter-dependent**; a failure in one weakens the entire UX.
- ✅ **Mnemonic:** **"U-D-F-A-C-V"** (Useful, Desirable, Findable, Accessible, Credible, Valuable) + **Usable** as the second U.
- ✅ **Distinguish in exams:** *Usability* (efficiency) $\neq$ *Accessibility* (inclusivity) $\neq$ *Desirability* (emotion).
- ✅ **Author Map:** Morville (UX Honeycomb) · Nielsen (Heuristics) · Norman (Emotional Design) · Garrett (Elements of UX) · W3C (WCAG).
- ✅ **Golden Formula for 14-mark answers:** Definition + Author + Year + Why-it-matters + Sub-criteria + Real-world Example + Measurement Method.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: What UX is Not
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECIT756 - USER INTERFACE AND USER EXPERIENCE DESIGN
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** What UX is Not

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

In the context of the **KTU 2024 Scheme** for *User Interface and User Experience Design*, the topic **"What UX is Not"** refers to a critical conceptual demarcation exercise. It is the systematic disambiguation of the term **User Experience (UX)** from other closely related, but fundamentally distinct, disciplines in the HCI (Human-Computer Interaction) ecosystem.

UX, as defined by **ISO 9241-210**, encompasses a person's *perceptions* and *responses* resulting from the use and/or anticipated use of a product, system, or service. The negative-definition approach (defining what UX is **not**) is a pedagogical and professional necessity to prevent scope creep, role confusion, and design myopia.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:**
> Module 2 of PECIT756 explicitly trains students to distinguish UX from **UI (User Interface)**, **Usability**, **HCI**, **Visual Design**, and **Information Architecture**. Examiners frequently test this boundary understanding using direct 3-mark definition questions and 7-mark comparison essays.

## 1.2 The Five Core Misconceptions (The "Anti-Definitions")

UX is **NOT** any single one of the following isolated disciplines:

1. **UX is NOT UI (User Interface)** — UI is the *visual and interactive layer*; UX is the *entire journey*.
2. **UX is NOT Usability** — Usability is a *quality attribute* (effectiveness, efficiency, satisfaction in a specific context); UX is the *holistic experience umbrella*.
3. **UX is NOT Visual Design** — Visual design concerns aesthetics, typography, color theory; UX is *problem-solving*.
4. **UX is NOT Coding / Development** — Implementation is downstream; UX is *upstream* research and strategy.
5. **UX is NOT Product Design or Branding** — These are adjacent business functions with different end goals.

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **The Restaurant Analogy:**
> Imagine a restaurant. **Visual Design** is the plating, color, and presentation of the food. **UI** is the menu card you hold in your hand. **Usability** is whether you can easily read the menu and figure out how to order. **UX** is the *entire experience* — the warmth of the welcome, the aroma when you walk in, the background music, the comfort of the chair, the taste of the food, the speed of service, and even the memory of the meal days later.
>
> If you only have a beautiful menu (UI) but cold food and rude waiters, your **UX is terrible** even though your **UI is gorgeous**.

## 1.4 Key Standard Metrics (KTU-Recognized)

While UX is qualitative, KTU-accepted measurable proxies include:

- **System Usability Scale (SUS)** — Standard score from **0 to 100** (a usability *component* of UX).
- **Net Promoter Score (NPS)** — Measures user loyalty; range **−100 to +100**.
- **Task Completion Rate** — Expressed as a **percentage (%)**.
- **Time-on-Task** — Measured in **seconds** or **milliseconds**.
- **Error Rate** — Expressed as **errors per task** or **percentage of users failing**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> These metrics measure *components* of UX, not UX itself. This distinction is a frequent KTU exam trap.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Comparison Sheet

## 2.1 The "Why" Behind the Negative Definition

The KTU 2024 scheme adopts Bloom's Taxonomy under NEP 2020. This topic sits at the **"Analyze" (Level 4)** cognitive level — students must *differentiate*, *compare*, and *contrast*. A positive-only definition of UX is too abstract for first-semester learners; the negative-definition pedagogy forces **boundary mapping**, a skill required in design audits and project scoping.

## 2.2 Operational Breakdown — The 5 Anti-Definitions

### 2.2.1 UX is NOT UI

| Aspect | UI (User Interface) | UX (User Experience) |
|---|---|---|
| **Scope** | Single screen or interaction point | End-to-end user journey |
| **Deliverable** | Wireframes, mockups, style guides | User flows, personas, journey maps, prototypes |
| **Focus** | Look, feel, interactivity | Meaning, value, emotion, task completion |
| **Question Asked** | "How should it look?" | "Why should it exist for the user?" |
| **Timing in SDLC** | Mid-to-late stage (design) | Early stage (research) → entire lifecycle |

> [!NOTE]
> **The Relationship:** UI is a *subset* of UX. You can have UX without polished UI (think early prototypes), but you cannot have meaningful UI without underlying UX thinking.

### 2.2.2 UX is NOT Usability

The **ISO 9241-11** standard defines usability through three sub-attributes:
- **Effectiveness** — Can the user achieve their goal?
- **Efficiency** — How much effort is required?
- **Satisfaction** — Was the experience comfortable and acceptable?

Usability is a **necessary but insufficient** condition for good UX. UX additionally includes:
- **Utility** — Does the product do something *useful* at all?
- **Desirability** — Does the user *want* to use it?
- **Accessibility** — Can *all* users (including those with disabilities) use it?
- **Emotional response** — Joy, trust, delight, frustration.
- **Brand perception** — What does using this product *say* about the user?

### 2.2.3 UX is NOT Visual Design

Visual design answers: *"Is it beautiful?"*
UX answers: *"Does it solve the right problem for the right person?"*

A visually stunning app with a broken checkout flow is a **visual design success** but a **UX failure**. Conversely, an ugly but functional tool (e.g., early GIMP, command-line tools) can be a **UX success** for its power-user base.

### 2.2.4 UX is NOT Coding

UX is a *research-driven, design-thinking discipline*. Coding is an *engineering discipline*. While UX designers benefit from HTML/CSS literacy and designers/prototypers may use tools like **Figma, Adobe XD, Axure, Balsamiq**, they are not expected to write production back-end code. Conversely, developers benefit from UX empathy but are not UX designers.

### 2.2.5 UX is NOT Product Design or Branding

- **Product Design** focuses on the *product's market fit and business viability*.
- **Branding** focuses on the *emotional and verbal identity* of the company.
- **UX** focuses on the *user's lived experience* with the product.

These three overlap in **Venn-diagram fashion**, but each has a distinct center of gravity: **Business**, **Identity**, and **User**, respectively.

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Misconception | One-Line Correction (Board-Ready) | KTU Cognitive Level |
|---|---|---|
| "UX = UI" | UX is the journey; UI is one touchpoint within that journey. | Understand (L2) |
| "UX = Usability" | Usability is a measurable quality attribute; UX is the holistic experience. | Analyze (L4) |
| "UX = Visual Design" | Visual design addresses aesthetics; UX addresses problem-solving and value. | Analyze (L4) |
| "UX = Coding" | UX is upstream research/design; coding is downstream implementation. | Understand (L2) |
| "UX = Branding" | UX is user-centric; branding is identity-centric. | Analyze (L4) |
| "UX = Product Design" | Product design is business-centric; UX is experience-centric. | Analyze (L4) |
| "UX = Making things pretty" | UX is making things *useful, usable, and desirable* — pretty is secondary. | Apply (L3) |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Real-World Engineering Utility:** In industry, conflating these roles leads to **project failures**, **scope creep**, **budget overruns**, and **user churn**. Startups frequently make the error of hiring "UI designers" and expecting "UX outcomes" — a mismatch that costs months of rework.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Analytical Matrix & Boundary Mapping

## 3.1 The 7-Stage Boundary-Mapping Procedure

This is the **KTU-recommended analytical workflow** for distinguishing UX from its neighbors. Use this verbatim in 14-mark essay questions.

### Step 1: Identify the Discipline in Question
**Conversion logic:** Begin by naming the two (or more) disciplines being compared. Naming them explicitly demonstrates taxonomic awareness.

$$\text{Disciplines}_{\text{compared}} = \{D_1, D_2, \ldots, D_n\}$$

For this topic:
$$D = \{UX, UI, Usability, Visual Design, Coding, Branding, Product Design\}$$

### Step 2: Define Each Discipline Using a Standard
**Conversion logic:** Reference an ISO standard, a foundational author, or an industry-accepted definition. This earns the **"academic rigor"** mark in KTU valuation.

| Discipline | Standard / Source | Core Definition |
|---|---|---|
| UX | ISO 9241-210 | Person's perceptions and responses from use of a product |
| Usability | ISO 9241-11 | Effectiveness, efficiency, satisfaction in a context of use |
| UI | Nielsen Norman Group | The visual and interactive layer through which a user interacts |
| Visual Design | Interaction Design Foundation | The aesthetic organization of visual elements |
| Coding | IEEE / ACM | Implementation of software logic in a programming language |
| Branding | American Marketing Association | The identity, promise, and personality of an organization |
| Product Design | IDEO methodology | The process of solving a real user problem in a viable way |

### Step 3: Identify the Core Question Each Discipline Asks
**Conversion logic:** Every design discipline is anchored to a unique guiding question. Mapping these questions reveals non-overlap.

$$\text{Core Question}(D_i) = \text{argmax}_{\text{perspective}} \text{Value}(D_i)$$

| Discipline | Core Question |
|---|---|
| UX | *"What is the user's whole journey and emotional arc?"* |
| UI | *"How should each screen look and feel?"* |
| Usability | *"How easily can a user accomplish a specific task?"* |
| Visual Design | *"Is it aesthetically pleasing and brand-aligned?"* |
| Coding | *"How do we build this technically and efficiently?"* |
| Branding | *"What does the company *feel* like to the world?"* |
| Product Design | *"Is this product viable, feasible, and desirable for the market?"* |

### Step 4: Map Overlap Zones
**Conversion logic:** Calculate the conceptual intersection. In KTU answers, a Venn diagram earns full marks.

Let $A, B$ be two disciplines. The overlap region is:
$$A \cap B = \{x \mid x \in A \land x \in B\}$$

In practice:
$$UX \cap UI = \{\text{Interaction Design, Microcopy, Information Architecture}\}$$
$$UX \cap Usability = \{\text{Ease of Use, Learnability, Error Prevention}\}$$
$$UX \cap Visual Design = \{\text{Visual Hierarchy, Layout Consistency, Color Semantics}\}$$
$$UX \cap Branding = \{\text{Tone of Voice, Brand-aligned Microcopy, Trust Signals}\}$$
$$UX \cap Product Design = \{\text{User Research, Problem Framing, Prototyping}\}$$

### Step 5: Identify the Unique-to-UX Domain
**Conversion logic:** What does UX *own* that no other discipline owns?

$$UX \setminus \bigcup_{i=1}^{n-1} D_i = \{\text{Journey Mapping, Empathy Research, Peak-End Rule Application, Emotional Design}\}$$

These four are **uniquely UX** and cannot be claimed by UI, usability, coding, or branding alone.

### Step 6: Construct a Contrast Table
**Conversion logic:** Tables earn the **"synthesis"** mark in KTU valuation. This is non-negotiable in 14-mark answers.

| Criterion | UX | UI | Usability | Visual Design |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Primary Goal | Holistic value | Interface clarity | Task efficiency | Aesthetic appeal |
| Output | Strategy, journey | Screens, components | Heuristics, test reports | Style guide, mocks |
| Method | Research, testing | Component design | Empirical testing | Composition, typography |
| Metric | NPS, SUS, CSAT | Component adoption | Task success rate | Brand recall |
| Stage | Early to late | Mid to late | Throughout | Mid to late |

### Step 7: State the Non-Negotiable Conclusion
**Conversion logic:** End with a one-sentence synthesis. This is the **"closing statement"** that KTU examiners specifically look for.

> **Conclusion Statement (Board-Ready):**
> *"UX is a multi-disciplinary umbrella that incorporates UI, usability, and visual design as essential but non-sufficient components. Conflating UX with any single one of these disciplines reduces it to a fragment and undermines its strategic, user-centered mandate."*

## 3.2 The Boundary-Mapping Visual Schema (Conceptual)

While a Venn diagram is the gold standard, here is the logical structure:

- **UX** = the *outermost circle* (largest scope)
- **Usability** = a *medium circle* inside UX
- **UI** = a *medium circle* inside UX
- **Visual Design** = a *small circle* inside UI
- **Coding** = *outside UX* (downstream dependency)
- **Branding** = *partially overlapping* with UX
- **Product Design** = *partially overlapping* with UX

This nesting is a recurring KTU question pattern.

> [!NOTE]
> **Examiner's Heuristic:** When in doubt, the KTU valuer assumes UX is the *superset*. If your answer places UX as a *subset* of any other discipline, expect to lose 2-3 marks immediately.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Diagram 1 — The UX Anti-Definition Tree

This diagram maps out the five primary "UX is Not" branches, suitable for 7-mark explanation questions.

```mermaid
graph TD
    root["UX IS NOT"]
    root --> branch1["UI"]
    root --> branch2["Usability"]
    root --> branch3["Visual Design"]
    root --> branch4["Coding"]
    root --> branch5["Branding and Product Design"]
    branch1 --> sub1a["UI is the touchpoint"]
    branch1 --> sub1b["UX is the journey"]
    branch2 --> sub2a["Usability is a quality attribute"]
    branch2 --> sub2b["UX is the holistic experience"]
    branch3 --> sub3a["Visual Design is aesthetics"]
    branch3 --> sub3b["UX is problem solving"]
    branch4 --> sub4a["Coding is implementation"]
    branch4 --> sub4b["UX is research and strategy"]
    branch5 --> sub5a["Branding is identity"]
    branch5 --> sub5b["Product Design is business viability"]
    branch5 --> sub5c["UX is user centric lived experience"]
```

## 4.2 Mermaid Diagram 2 — The Nested Scope Model

This diagram visualizes UX as the superset umbrella containing its sub-disciplines.

```mermaid
graph TD
    outer["UX - User Experience - The Umbrella"]
    outer --> middle1["Usability - Quality Attribute"]
    outer --> middle2["UI - User Interface"]
    outer --> middle3["Information Architecture"]
    outer --> middle4["Interaction Design"]
    outer --> middle5["Content Strategy"]
    outer --> outer2["Adjacent Disciplines - Partial Overlap"]
    outer2 --> adj1["Branding - Identity"]
    outer2 --> adj2["Product Design - Business Viability"]
    outer2 --> adj3["Marketing - Acquisition"]
    middle2 --> inner1["Visual Design - Aesthetics"]
    middle2 --> inner2["Microcopy and Tone"]
    middle2 --> inner3["Component Library"]
```

## 4.3 Mermaid Diagram 3 — The Boundary-Mapping Flow

This block-level functional architecture illustrates how a UX decision flows through (and excludes) adjacent disciplines.

```mermaid
graph TD
    start["User Need Identified"]
    research["UX Research - Interviews, Surveys, Ethnography"]
    strategy["UX Strategy - Personas, Journey Maps, Problem Framing"]
    scope{"Is this a UX task?"}
    yes1["Proceed with UX Design"]
    no1["Route to Adjacent Discipline"]
    ia["Information Architecture"]
    id["Interaction Design"]
    wireframe["Wireframing and Prototyping"]
    usabilityT["Usability Testing"]
    handoff["Handoff to UI and Visual Designers"]
    dev["Handoff to Development and Coding"]
    launch["Product Launch and Continuous UX Iteration"]
    scope -- YES --> ia
    scope -- NO --> no1
    ia --> id
    id --> wireframe
    wireframe --> usabilityT
    usabilityT --> handoff
    handoff --> dev
    dev --> launch
    launch -. feedback loop .-> research
    no1 --> adjDis["Branding, Marketing, or Product Strategy Teams"]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Interpretation Note for Students:**
> In Diagram 3, the decision diamond `Is this a UX task?` is the **core gatekeeper** of the "What UX is Not" philosophy. Many features (e.g., choosing a brand color palette, picking a tech stack, writing SEO copy) are *not* UX tasks — they belong to adjacent disciplines. A mature UX designer routes these correctly rather than absorbing them into UX scope.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: Define UX. List any three things that UX is NOT.
**[KTU University Exam — June 2024 Model Question]**

**Model Answer:**

**Definition (2 marks):**
User Experience (UX) is defined by **ISO 9241-210** as a person's *perceptions* and *responses* resulting from the use and/or anticipated use of a product, system, or service. It is a holistic, user-centered discipline that spans the entire interaction journey.

**Three things UX is NOT (1 mark — 0.33 each):**
1. UX is **not** the same as **UI (User Interface)**. UI is the visual and interactive layer; UX is the entire user journey.
2. UX is **not** the same as **Usability**. Usability is a measurable quality attribute (effectiveness, efficiency, satisfaction), while UX is the broader experience.
3. UX is **not** the same as **Visual Design**. Visual design addresses aesthetics; UX addresses problem-solving and value delivery.

---

### Question 2: Differentiate between UX and UI in two points.
**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Model Question]**

**Model Answer:**

| Aspect | UX (User Experience) | UI (User Interface) |
|---|---|---|
| **Scope** | The entire user journey from discovery to post-use | The specific visual and interactive elements of a screen |
| **Focus** | Solving the right problem for the right user | Presenting the solution in a clear, attractive, and interactive way |

> [!NOTE]
> **UI is a *subset* of UX.** All UI work happens within a UX context, but UX extends far beyond any single interface.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks) — Internal Choice Format

### Question A (14 Marks) — Option 1

**[KTU University Exam — June 2024 Model Question]**

**(a)** *"User Experience is often misunderstood as being the same as User Interface Design."* Critically analyze this statement, distinguishing UX from UI with suitable examples. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Explain how UX differs from **Usability**, **Visual Design**, and **Coding**. Use a comparison table to support your answer. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution for Question A(a):

**1. Introduction (1 mark):**
The statement is **incorrect**. UX and UI are related but fundamentally different disciplines. UI is a *component* of UX, not a synonym for it.

**2. Definition of UI (1.5 marks):**
UI (User Interface) refers to the **visual, interactive, and informational elements** through which a user communicates with a product. It includes buttons, icons, typography, color, spacing, microcopy, and feedback animations. UI designers focus on **layout consistency, component reusability, and visual hierarchy**.

**3. Definition of UX (1.5 marks):**
UX (User Experience) refers to the **holistic experience** a user has with a product, system, or service. Per ISO 9241-210, it encompasses all perceptions, emotions, and responses — including those *before*, *during*, and *after* use. UX encompasses research, strategy, information architecture, interaction design, and usability.

**4. Key Differences (2 marks):**

| Aspect | UI | UX |
|---|---|---|
| **Scope** | Single screen or touchpoint | End-to-end user journey |
| **Question Asked** | "How should it look?" | "Why should this exist for the user?" |
| **Output** | Mockups, style guides, components | Personas, journey maps, prototypes, research reports |
| **Stage in Process** | Mid-to-late design phase | Begins in research, continues post-launch |

**5. Illustrative Example (1 mark):**
Consider **Amazon's checkout flow**. The *UI* of the checkout page includes the orange "Buy Now" button, the address form fields, and the credit-card input layout. The *UX* encompasses the entire purchase journey: the personalized product recommendations (research-informed), the one-click ordering (efficiency-driven), the order-tracking page (post-purchase trust), and the easy return policy (anxiety reduction). A beautifully designed checkout page with confusing navigation is a UI success and a UX failure.

**6. Conclusion (board-closing, 0.5 mark):**
Thus, UI is a *necessary subset* of UX. Good UX may exist with rough UI (early prototypes), but good UI without thoughtful UX is merely decorative.

---

#### Model Solution for Question A(b):

**1. Introduction (1 mark):**
UX is frequently conflated with three neighboring disciplines: **Usability**, **Visual Design**, and **Coding**. Each is essential, but none is equivalent to UX.

**2. UX vs Usability (2 marks):**
**Usability** (ISO 9241-11) is a *quality attribute* with three measurable components: **effectiveness, efficiency, and satisfaction**. UX is the *umbrella* under which usability sits, alongside utility, desirability, accessibility, and emotional response. A product can be highly usable (efficient checkout) but offer a poor UX (if the product itself is unwanted or the brand experience is hostile).

**3. UX vs Visual Design (2 marks):**
**Visual Design** concerns aesthetics — color, typography, imagery, and composition. It answers *"Is it beautiful?"*. UX answers *"Is it useful, usable, and meaningful?"*. A visually stunning app that solves no real problem is a visual design success and a UX failure. Conversely, a utilitarian, ugly tool (e.g., `grep` or `vim`) can be a UX triumph for its target audience.

**4. UX vs Coding (1.5 marks):**
**Coding** is the *implementation* of design in a programming language. UX is the *upstream* activity of research, strategy, and design. UX designers produce *specifications*; developers produce *functional code*. Both are necessary, but they are sequential, not interchangeable.

**5. Comparison Table (essential for full marks — 0.5 mark deducted if missing):**

| Criterion | UX | Usability | Visual Design | Coding |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Primary Goal** | Holistic value | Task efficiency | Aesthetic appeal | Functional implementation |
| **Stage in SDLC** | Early to late | Throughout | Mid to late | Mid to late |
| **Key Metric** | NPS, SUS, CSAT | Task success rate, time-on-task | Brand recall, visual consistency | Code coverage, performance |
| **Deliverable** | Personas, journey maps, prototypes | Heuristic reports, test results | Mockups, style guides | Working software |

**6. Conclusion (board-closing, 0.5 mark):**
UX is the *integrator* of these disciplines. It does not replace them; it coordinates them in service of the user's holistic experience.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Option 2

**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Model Question]**

**(a)** *"UX is much broader than Usability."* Justify this statement with reference to ISO standards and at least three components of UX that are outside the scope of Usability. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Compare and contrast UX with **Branding** and **Product Design**. Why is it a mistake to use these terms interchangeably? **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution for Question B(a):

**1. Introduction (1 mark):**
The statement is **correct and well-established** in the HCI literature. Usability is a *subset* of UX, defined narrowly by ISO 9241-11, while UX is defined broadly by ISO 9241-210.

**2. ISO Definitions (2 marks):**

**Usability (ISO 9241-11):**
$$\text{Usability} = f(\text{Effectiveness}, \text{Efficiency}, \text{Satisfaction}) \mid \text{in a specific context of use}$$

**UX (ISO 9241-210):**
$$\text{UX} = \{\text{perceptions}, \text{responses}\} \mid \text{from use and/or anticipated use}$$

The UX definition explicitly encompasses *anticipated* use (i.e., expectations, pre-use emotions) and *all* responses (cognitive, emotional, physical), which go far beyond the three usability components.

**3. Three Components of UX Outside Usability (3 marks — 1 each):**

**(i) Utility** — Does the product do something *useful* at all? A usable calculator that no one needs has high usability and low UX value.

**(ii) Desirability** — Does the user *want* to use the product? This includes emotional appeal, brand affinity, and marketing perception. Usability does not measure desire.

**(iii) Emotional Response & Trust** — Feelings of joy, anxiety, frustration, trust, and delight that emerge from use. Usability can be high (task completed) while the user feels frustrated or anxious (e.g., a usable but manipulative dark-pattern interface).

**Additional acceptable components:** Accessibility, brand perception, peak-end emotional memory, social signaling, cultural resonance.

**4. Illustrative Example (1 mark):**
Consider **Apple iPhone vs a feature phone with a calculator, flashlight, and long battery life**. The feature phone may have *higher usability* for its narrow tasks, but the iPhone delivers a vastly superior *user experience* through desirability, ecosystem, emotional branding, and aspirational value.

**5. Conclusion (board-closing, 0.5 mark):**
Therefore, usability is a *necessary but insufficient* condition for good UX. A product can be usable and still deliver a poor user experience, just as a product can deliver excellent UX only when usability has been achieved.

---

#### Model Solution for Question B(b):

**1. Introduction (1 mark):**
Branding, Product Design, and UX are three *distinct but overlapping* disciplines. Using them interchangeably is a common but consequential error in industry and academia.

**2. UX vs Branding (2.5 marks):**

| Aspect | UX | Branding |
|---|---|---|
| **Center of Gravity** | The user | The organization / company |
| **Goal** | Solve a user problem and deliver a valuable experience | Build a recognizable, consistent identity and emotional promise |
| **Question Asked** | "What does the user need and feel?" | "What should the company stand for?" |
| **Deliverables** | Personas, journey maps, prototypes | Logo, brand guidelines, voice and tone, visual identity |
| **Success Metric** | NPS, SUS, task success | Brand recall, brand equity, brand sentiment |
| **Example** | Redesigning the checkout flow to reduce cart abandonment | Deciding that the brand voice is "playful and irreverent" |

The overlap zone is *brand-aligned microcopy*, *tone of voice in error messages*, and *visual consistency across touchpoints* — but the strategic intent differs.

**3. UX vs Product Design (2.5 marks):**

| Aspect | UX | Product Design |
|---|---|---|
| **Center of Gravity** | The user | The product and the business |
| **Goal** | Optimize the user experience | Make the product viable, feasible, and desirable for the market |
| **Framework** | Design Thinking, Lean UX, Jobs-to-be-Done | Business Model Canvas, MVP, Agile, GTM Strategy |
| **Question Asked** | "How should this feel and work for the user?" | "Should this product exist, and can it succeed in the market?" |
| **Deliverables** | Wireframes, user flows, prototypes | Business plans, roadmaps, feature prioritization matrices |

**4. Why the Confusion is Dangerous (1 mark):**
Conflating these terms leads to:
- **Branding teams** being asked to deliver UX outcomes they are not equipped for.
- **Product Managers** making user-research decisions without UX research rigor.
- **UX Designers** being held accountable for business viability metrics outside their control.

**5. Conclusion (board-closing, 0.5 mark):**
UX, Branding, and Product Design are *complementary but non-substitutable*. A successful product requires excellence in all three, executed by professionals with distinct — and occasionally overlapping — skill sets.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Do NOT write "UX is the same as UI"** even casually — KTU examiners auto-deduct 1-2 marks for this in 14-mark answers.
> 2. **Do NOT omit the comparison table** in 7-mark sub-questions comparing two or more disciplines. Tables are worth at least 0.5-1 mark and are non-negotiable.
> 3. **Do NOT confuse "Usability" with "UX"** in Part A. The 3-mark question "Define UX" is *not* answered by reciting the ISO 9241-11 usability definition.
> 4. **Do NOT skip the conclusion sentence** in 14-mark answers. The closing statement is a specific marker KTU examiners are trained to look for.
> 5. **Do NOT use "UX/UI" as a single slash-term** — the KTU 2024 syllabus explicitly penalizes this conflation. Always write "UX and UI" or "UX, which is distinct from UI".
> 6. **Do NOT forget to cite ISO standards** (9241-210 for UX, 9241-11 for Usability) — this is the academic rigor marker worth 0.5-1 mark per reference.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Summary for Last-Minute Revision:**

- ✅ UX is the **holistic, end-to-end experience**; UI is **one touchpoint within that experience**.
- ✅ UX is defined by **ISO 9241-210**; Usability is defined by **ISO 9241-11** — cite both.
- ✅ Usability = **Effectiveness + Efficiency + Satisfaction** (3 measurable components).
- ✅ UX also includes **Utility, Desirability, Accessibility, Emotional Response, Brand Perception** (5+ additional components).
- ✅ UI is a **subset** of UX; Visual Design is a **subset** of UI; Coding is **downstream** of UX, not a synonym.
- ✅ **Branding** is identity-centric; **Product Design** is business-centric; **UX** is user-centric.
- ✅ UX is the **outermost circle** (superset); Usability and UI are **inner circles** (subsets).
- ✅ Conflating UX with UI is the **#1 KTU exam trap** — always distinguish them.
- ✅ Always include a **comparison table** in 7-mark comparison questions.
- ✅ Always end 14-mark answers with a **one-line conclusion** stating the non-overlap.
- ✅ **Visual Design** answers *"Is it beautiful?"*; UX answers *"Is it useful, usable, and meaningful?"*
- ✅ **Coding** is implementation; UX is research, strategy, and design — they are sequential, not interchangeable.
- ✅ The **peak-end rule** (Kahneman) is a uniquely UX concept — emotional memory is formed by the peak moment and the final moment, not the average.
- ✅ Accessibility is a **right, not a feature** — KTU 2024 explicitly includes it in UX scope.
- ✅ **Design Thinking** (Empathize, Define, Ideate, Prototype, Test) is a UX process — not a branding or product process.
- ✅ Personas, Journey Maps, and Empathy Maps are **uniquely UX deliverables** — no other discipline produces them as primary outputs.
- ✅ **System Usability Scale (SUS)** score ranges from **0 to 100**; a score above **68** is considered above-average.
- ✅ **Net Promoter Score (NPS)** ranges from **−100 to +100** and measures user loyalty (a UX outcome).
- ✅ Always remember: **UX is a way of thinking, not a deliverable.** It is a discipline, a culture, and a process — not a single artifact.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Kinds of Interaction and UX
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECIT756 - USER INTERFACE AND USER EXPERIENCE DESIGN
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Kinds of Interaction and UX

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Aligned)

In Human-Computer Interaction (HCI) and User Experience (UX) design, **Kinds of Interaction** refers to the **taxonomy of communication styles** that a user employs to converse with, instruct, manipulate, or explore an interactive system. These interaction styles are the **fundamental dialogue modes** through which user input is captured, interpreted, and translated into system behaviour.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Definition (Verbatim-aligned):**
> *"Kinds of Interaction describe the various paradigms through which users communicate their intent to a system — including instructing, conversing, manipulating, and exploring — and each paradigm directly shapes the quality of the User Experience (UX) delivered."*

**User Experience (UX)**, as defined by the **ISO 9241-210 standard**, is a *"person's perceptions and responses that result from the use or anticipated use of a product, system or service."* It is the **holistic** emotional, behavioural, and cognitive outcome of every interaction.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Think of visiting a **new city for the first time**. You can:

- **Read a guidebook** and give yourself instructions (like **Command-Line / Instructing**).
- **Hire a local guide** and ask questions, have a dialogue (like **Conversing / Chatbots**).
- **Walk freely and touch things**, picking up objects to understand them (like **Direct Manipulation**).
- **Stroll through an open museum**, discovering rooms on your own (like **Exploring / Hypertext**).

> [!NOTE]
> Just as your overall **holiday experience** is determined by *how* you interacted with the city (the sights, the local food, the ease of moving around, the warmth of the guide), a digital product's **UX is determined by the kind of interaction style** the designer chose for the user. A "good holiday" requires the right balance of guidance and freedom — exactly what UX design aims to achieve.

## 1.3 Core Categorical Anchors

The four canonical **kinds of interaction** (per Preece, Rogers & Sharp's HCI textbook, which is the standard reference for KTU UI/UX modules) are:

| # | Interaction Style | Mental Model |
|---|-------------------|--------------|
| 1 | **Instructing** | User issues precise commands |
| 2 | **Conversing** | User "talks" to the system |
| 3 | **Manipulating** | User acts on objects in a virtual space |
| 4 | **Exploring** | User navigates an information environment |

The **UX outcome** is the **sum total** of the user's perceptions across all four kinds.

## 1.4 Quick-Reference Definition Box

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Term — Interaction Paradigm:**
> A *paradigm* is a foundational style of dialogue. KTU 2024 scheme explicitly asks students to identify *which paradigm* a given application uses and to *justify the UX implications* of that choice.
>
> **Key Term — UX (User Experience):**
> UX ≠ UI. **UI (User Interface)** is the *screen*, the *buttons*, the *visual surface*. **UX** is the *complete journey* — emotions, efficiency, errors, satisfaction, accessibility, and trust.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Four Kinds of Interaction — Exhaustive Breakdown

### 2.1.1 Instructing (Command-Based Interaction)

- **Operational Logic:** The user issues **explicit, syntax-bound commands** to a system. The system is passive and only acts upon receiving a valid instruction.
- **Historical Examples:** MS-DOS, UNIX shells, AutoCAD command palette.
- **UX Strengths:** Precision, repeatability, scriptability, low memory overhead.
- **UX Weaknesses:** Steep learning curve, high memory load, unforgiving error handling.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU High-Yield Note:** *Instructing* is still preferred for **expert users, power tools, and accessibility scenarios (e.g., screen readers for visually impaired users).*

### 2.1.2 Conversing (Dialogue-Based Interaction)

- **Operational Logic:** The user and system engage in a **bidirectional exchange**, often using natural language (typed or spoken).
- **Sub-categories:**
  * **Question-and-Answer** (early expert systems like MYCIN).
  * **Form-filling dialogue** (banking portals, job applications).
  * **Natural Language / Conversational Agents** (ChatGPT, Alexa, Siri).
- **UX Strengths:** Low learning curve, accessibility-friendly, mimics human-to-human communication.
- **UX Weaknesses:** Ambiguity handling, latency, frustration when intent is misunderstood.

### 2.1.3 Manipulating (Direct Manipulation Interaction)

- **Operational Logic:** The user **directly acts on visible objects** in a virtual workspace. Ben Shneiderman (1983) coined the term, identifying three pillars:
  1. **Continuous representation** of the object of interest.
  2. **Physical actions** (drag, pinch, rotate) instead of complex syntax.
  3. **Rapid, incremental, reversible** operations whose impact on the object is immediately visible.
- **Examples:** macOS Finder drag-and-drop, Figma canvas, Tilt Brush.
- **UX Strengths:** High engagement, low cognitive load, immediate feedback.
- **UX Weaknesses:** Demands screen real estate, may not scale to data-heavy tasks.

> [!NOTE]
> **Ben Shneiderman's Three Pillars of Direct Manipulation are a recurring KTU exam topic.** Memorise them verbatim.

### 2.1.4 Exploring (Navigational / Hypertext Interaction)

- **Operational Logic:** The user **moves through** an information space via links, breadcrumbs, or spatial navigation. Cognition happens through **navigation and discovery**.
- **Examples:** Wikipedia, museum kiosks, e-learning platforms, the World Wide Web itself.
- **UX Strengths:** Suits non-linear information, supports serendipity.
- **UX Weaknesses:** "Lost in hyperspace" syndrome, poor information scent, disorientation.

## 2.2 The UX Honeycomb — Peter Morville's Framework

Peter Morville (2004) visualised UX as a **honeycomb of seven faceted qualities**. Every interaction style must be evaluated against these:

- **Useful** — does it solve a real problem?
- **Usable** — can the user accomplish goals easily?
- **Findable** — can the user locate what they need?
- **Credible** — does the user trust the system?
- **Desirable** — is the design emotionally appealing?
- **Accessible** — can users with disabilities use it?
- **Valuable** — does it deliver value to the business?

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Hot Topic:** The honeycomb is the **most asked UX framework** in Part B questions. You must be able to *map* any interaction style to all seven facets and *critique* it.

## 2.3 Don Norman's Seven Principles of Interaction Design

Norman (1988) defined the **gulfs of execution and evaluation** and the **seven principles** that bridge them:

1. **Visibility** — system status and affordances must be visible.
2. **Feedback** — full, continuous feedback for every action.
3. **Constraints** — limit possible actions to prevent error.
4. **Mapping** — natural relationship between controls and effects.
5. **Consistency** — same operation → same result everywhere.
6. **Affordance** — visual cues suggest how an object is used.
7. **Error prevention / recovery** — design for the worst case.

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet (UX Metrics)

| Metric | Formula | Units | Use Case |
|--------|---------|-------|----------|
| **Task Completion Rate (TCR)** | $TCR = \dfrac{T_c}{T_t} \times 100$ | **%** | % of users who complete a task |
| **Time on Task (ToT)** | $ToT = T_{end} - T_{start}$ | **seconds** | Efficiency measure |
| **Error Rate (ER)** | $ER = \dfrac{E}{A} \times 100$ | **%** | Mistakes per attempt |
| **System Usability Scale (SUS)** | $\sum (Score_i \times 2.5)$ | **0–100** | Standardised usability score |
| **Learnability Index (LI)** | $LI = \dfrac{T_1}{T_n}$ | **ratio** | Time to first success vs. nth success |
| **Hick's Law (Decision Time)** | $T = a + b \cdot \log_2(n+1)$ | **ms** | Time to choose among $n$ options |
| **Fitts's Law (Movement Time)** | $MT = a + b \cdot \log_2\!\left(\dfrac{D}{W}+1\right)$ | **ms** | Time to acquire a target of width $W$ at distance $D$ |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Hick's Law and Fitts's Law are mathematical laws of interaction.** In KTU, you may be asked to compute decision time for a menu of $n$ items, or movement time for a button of width $W$ at distance $D$. **Memorise both formulas.**

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Instructing** is used in **DevOps pipelines**, **CLI tools**, and **embedded firmware** debug consoles.
- **Conversing** powers **call-centre IVRs**, **voice-first smart homes**, and **LLM-based copilots**.
- **Manipulating** dominates **CAD/CAM systems**, **video editing software**, and **AR/VR interfaces**.
- **Exploring** is core to **E-commerce catalogues**, **digital libraries**, and **GIS mapping systems**.

The choice of interaction kind is a **strategic UX decision** — not a stylistic preference. It must align with user expertise, task complexity, context of use, and accessibility requirements.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Exhaustive Worked Derivation — Applying Hick's Law

**Problem Statement:**
A smartphone launcher presents the user with **8 apps** in a grid. Compute the user's average decision time to pick one app, given $a = 150 \text{ ms}$ and $b = 200 \text{ ms}$ per binary decision.

### Step-by-Step Solution

**Step 1 — Identify the parameters.**
Number of choices $n = 8$.
Hick's Law constants: $a = 150 \text{ ms}$, $b = 200 \text{ ms/bit}$.

**Step 2 — Convert the count of choices to bits of information.**
Using the information-theoretic principle, the number of binary decisions required to uniquely identify one of $n$ choices is:

$$
H = \log_2(n+1)
$$

$$
H = \log_2(8+1) = \log_2(9)
$$

**Step 3 — Evaluate the logarithm.**

$$
\log_2(9) = \dfrac{\ln(9)}{\ln(2)} = \dfrac{2.1972}{0.6931} \approx 3.1699 \text{ bits}
$$

**Step 4 — Substitute into Hick's Law.**

$$
T = a + b \cdot \log_2(n+1)
$$

$$
T = 150 + 200 \times 3.1699
$$

$$
T = 150 + 633.98 = 783.98 \text{ ms}
$$

**Step 5 — Interpretation.**
The user will take approximately **$\mathbf{784 \text{ ms}}$** to decide which app to tap. Designers can **reduce this latency** by grouping apps into folders (reducing effective $n$) or by surfacing the most likely choice via **predictive suggestions** (machine-learning ranking).

## 3.2 Exhaustive Worked Derivation — Applying Fitts's Law

**Problem Statement:**
A "Buy Now" button on an e-commerce site is rectangular, **width $W = 80 \text{ px}$**, located **distance $D = 320 \text{ px}$** from the user's current cursor position. Given $a = 50 \text{ ms}$ and $b = 150 \text{ ms/bit}$, compute the movement time.

### Step-by-Step Solution

**Step 1 — Compute the index of difficulty (ID).**

$$
ID = \log_2\!\left(\dfrac{D}{W}+1\right)
$$

**Step 2 — Substitute the values.**

$$
ID = \log_2\!\left(\dfrac{320}{80}+1\right) = \log_2(4+1) = \log_2(5)
$$

**Step 3 — Evaluate the logarithm.**

$$
ID = \dfrac{\ln(5)}{\ln(2)} = \dfrac{1.6094}{0.6931} \approx 2.3219 \text{ bits}
$$

**Step 4 — Compute the movement time.**

$$
MT = a + b \cdot ID
$$

$$
MT = 50 + 150 \times 2.3219 = 50 + 348.29 = 398.29 \text{ ms}
$$

**Step 5 — Design recommendation.**
The acquisition takes roughly **$\mathbf{398 \text{ ms}}$**. To make this faster, **increase the button width** (e.g., make it $160$ px) or **reduce the distance** (place the button near the user's gaze point). This is the very reason "Buy Now" buttons on Amazon are **large, centrally located, and high-contrast** — a direct Fitts's Law application.

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Interaction Style Comparison

| Evaluation Dimension | Instructing | Conversing | Manipulating | Exploring |
|----------------------|-------------|------------|--------------|-----------|
| **Cognitive Load** | High (syntax recall) | Low–Medium | Low (intuitive) | Medium (orientation cost) |
| **Learning Curve** | Steep | Gentle | Gentle–Moderate | Moderate |
| **Error Tolerance** | Low | High (rephrasable) | High (reversible) | Variable |
| **Best User Profile** | Expert, Developer | Novice, Casual | Visual, Creative | Researcher, Browser |
| **Feedback Speed** | Instant (on valid input) | Latency-bound | Real-time, continuous | Page-/view-bound |
| **Accessibility** | Screen-reader friendly | Voice-control friendly | Motor-skill demanding | Variable |
| **Suitability for Mobile** | Poor | Excellent | Excellent (touch) | Good |
| **Discoverability of Features** | Poor (hidden in syntax) | Good (suggestions) | Excellent (visible) | Excellent (links) |
| **Scalability to Complex Tasks** | Excellent | Poor | Moderate | Moderate |
| **Memory Load on User** | Very High | Low | Very Low | Medium |

## 3.4 Mapping Interaction Kinds to UX Facets (Morville's Honeycomb)

| UX Facet | Instructing | Conversing | Manipulating | Exploring |
|----------|-------------|------------|--------------|-----------|
| **Useful** | High for pros | High for support | High for creation | High for discovery |
| **Usable** | Low for novices | High | Very High | Medium |
| **Findable** | Poor | Good (suggestions) | Good (visible UI) | Medium (depends on IA) |
| **Credible** | High (precise) | Variable | High (WYSIWYG) | Variable |
| **Desirable** | Low (intimidating) | High (human-like) | Very High (fun) | High (curiosity) |
| **Accessible** | High (text-based) | Very High (voice) | Limited (motor req.) | Medium |
| **Valuable** | High for automation | High for CX | High for engagement | High for content sites |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Diagram — The Four Kinds of Interaction Map

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    userA[User Intent] --> controller{Interaction Style Selector}
    controller -->|Precise syntax| instr[Instructing]
    controller -->|Natural dialogue| conv[Conversing]
    controller -->|Direct object action| manip[Manipulating]
    controller -->|Navigation and discovery| expl[Exploring]
    instr --> ux1[UX Outcome: Efficiency]
    conv --> ux2[UX Outcome: Accessibility]
    manip --> ux3[UX Outcome: Engagement]
    expl --> ux4[UX Outcome: Discovery]
    ux1 --> honey[UX Honeycomb Evaluation]
    ux2 --> honey
    ux3 --> honey
    ux4 --> honey
    honey --> judge{Is UX Acceptable?}
    judge -->|Yes| ship[Product is Shipped]
    judge -->|No| iter[Iterate Design]
    iter --> controller
```

## 4.2 Mermaid Diagram — Peter Morville's UX Honeycomb (Faceted View)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    centerA((UX Core)) --- useA[Useful]
    centerA --- usaA[Usable]
    centerA --- finA[Findable]
    centerA --- creA[Credible]
    centerA --- desA[Desirable]
    centerA --- accA[Accessible]
    centerA --- valA[Valuable]
    useA --- usaA
    usaA --- finA
    finA --- creA
    creA --- desA
    desA --- accA
    accA --- valA
    valA --- useA
```

## 4.3 Mermaid Diagram — Don Norman's Gulfs of Interaction (Block-Level Architecture)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    goalA[User Goal] --> formA[Formulate Intention]
    formA --> specA[Specify Action]
    specA --> gulfE{{Gulf of Execution}}
    gulfE --> sysActA[System Action]
    sysActA --> sysStA[System State Change]
    sysStA --> gulfV{{Gulf of Evaluation}}
    gulfV --> perceptA[Perceive Display]
    perceptA --> interpA[Interpret State]
    interpA --> compA[Compare to Goal]
    compA --> achieved{Goal Achieved?}
    achieved -->|Yes| endA[Task Complete]
    achieved -->|No| formA
```

## 4.4 Mermaid Diagram — Block-Level Functional Architecture of UX

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    userReqA[User Requirements] --> researchA[User Research]
    researchA --> personaA[Persona Definition]
    personaA --> uxGoalsA[UX Goals]
    uxGoalsA --> choiceA{Choose Interaction Style}
    choiceA --> designA[Wireframe and Prototype]
    designA --> usabilityA[Usability Test]
    usabilityA --> metricsA[Measure SUS, TCR, ToT]
    metricsA --> evalA{Evaluate vs Honeycomb}
    evalA -->|Pass| finalA[Final Product]
    evalA -->|Fail| redesignA[Redesign Loop]
    redesignA --> choiceA
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1. **[KTU University Exam — May 2024]**
**Explain the four kinds of interaction in HCI. Give one real-world example of each.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
The four kinds of interaction in HCI, as defined by Preece, Rogers and Sharp, are:

1. **Instructing** — The user issues precise, syntax-bound commands to the system. *Example:* using a UNIX terminal command such as `ls -la /home` to list directory contents.
2. **Conversing** — The user engages in a bidirectional dialogue with the system, often using natural language. *Example:* asking Alexa "What is the weather today?" and receiving a spoken reply.
3. **Manipulating** — The user directly acts on visible objects via physical gestures (drag, pinch, rotate) with immediate visual feedback. *Example:* dragging a file into a folder in macOS Finder.
4. **Exploring** — The user navigates an information environment via links and hyperlinks, building mental models through discovery. *Example:* browsing Wikipedia articles by following blue links across related topics.

Each style shapes UX differently — *Instructing* maximises efficiency, *Conversing* maximises accessibility, *Manipulating* maximises engagement, and *Exploring* maximises information discovery. **(3 marks)**

---

### Question 2. **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**
**Define User Experience (UX) and list any five facets of Peter Morville's UX Honeycomb.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
User Experience (UX), as defined by **ISO 9241-210**, is *"a person's perceptions and responses that result from the use or anticipated use of a product, system or service."* It encompasses all aspects of the user's interaction, including emotions, beliefs, preferences, perceptions, physical and psychological responses, behaviours, and accomplishments that occur before, during, and after use.

The **five facets of Peter Morville's UX Honeycomb** (out of seven) are:

1. **Useful** — the product must solve a real user problem.
2. **Usable** — the product must be easy to learn and use.
3. **Findable** — information and features must be easy to locate.
4. **Credible** — users must trust the product and its content.
5. **Accessible** — the product must be usable by people with diverse abilities and disabilities.

The remaining two facets are **Desirable** and **Valuable**. **(3 marks)**

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Pattern:** Each Part B question carries **14 marks** and must be answered as **two sub-parts of 7 marks each**. You may attempt **either** OR A **or** OR B.

---

### Question A (14 Marks) **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2024]**

**"Choose any two interaction styles and evaluate their impact on User Experience (UX) using Don Norman's principles and Peter Morville's Honeycomb."**

#### Part A(a) — Instructing vs. Conversing Interaction Styles (7 marks)

**Model Answer:**

**1. Instructing — Conceptual Description (2 marks):**
Instructing is a **command-based interaction style** where the user types or issues explicit, often syntax-bound commands. The system is passive, awaiting valid input. Examples include UNIX shells, SQL queries, and AutoCAD command palettes. UX strengths include **precision, repeatability, and scriptability**; UX weaknesses include a **steep learning curve, high memory load, and unforgiving error behaviour**.

**2. Conversing — Conceptual Description (2 marks):**
Conversing is a **dialogue-based interaction style** where the user and system exchange information bidirectionally, often in natural language. Examples include chatbots, voice assistants (Siri, Alexa), and form-filling dialogues. UX strengths include a **gentle learning curve, accessibility for non-technical users, and empathy-driven interaction**; UX weaknesses include **ambiguity, latency, and contextual misunderstanding**.

**3. UX Evaluation Using Morville's Honeycomb (2 marks):**

| Facet | Instructing | Conversing |
|-------|-------------|------------|
| Usable | Poor for novices | Excellent |
| Accessible | Good (text-based, screen-reader friendly) | Excellent (voice-based) |
| Credible | High (deterministic) | Variable |
| Findable | Poor (hidden in syntax) | Good (intent recognition) |
| Desirable | Low | High |

**4. Norman's Principle Mapping (1 mark):**
Instructing satisfies *Visibility* and *Consistency* well but fails *Affordance* and *Error Prevention*. Conversing satisfies *Error Recovery* and *Constraints* well but struggles with *Mapping* due to linguistic ambiguity.

#### Part A(b) — Manipulating vs. Exploring Interaction Styles (7 marks)

**Model Answer:**

**1. Manipulating — Direct Manipulation by Shneiderman (2 marks):**
Manipulating is a **direct-manipulation style** where the user acts on visible objects using physical gestures. Shneiderman's three pillars are: **(i)** continuous representation of the object, **(ii)** physical actions instead of complex syntax, and **(iii)** rapid, incremental, reversible operations with immediate feedback. UX strengths include **high engagement and low cognitive load**; weaknesses include **screen real-estate demand and motor-skill requirements**.

**2. Exploring — Navigational / Hypertext Interaction (2 marks):**
Exploring is a **navigational style** where the user moves through an information space via links, breadcrumbs, or spatial cues. UX strengths include **serendipity, non-linear learning, and breadth of discovery**; weaknesses include the **"lost in hyperspace" syndrome** and information disorientation.

**3. UX Evaluation Using Morville's Honeycomb (2 marks):**

| Facet | Manipulating | Exploring |
|-------|--------------|-----------|
| Useful | High for creation | High for discovery |
| Usable | Very High (intuitive) | Medium (orientation cost) |
| Findable | Good (visible objects) | Medium (depends on IA) |
| Desirable | Very High (WYSIWYG fun) | High (curiosity-driven) |
| Accessible | Limited (motor demand) | Medium |

**4. Norman's Principle Mapping (1 mark):**
Manipulating excels at *Visibility*, *Feedback*, and *Affordance*. Exploring excels at *Mapping* (when information architecture is sound) but struggles with *Error Prevention* because navigation errors are silent.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) **[KTU University Exam — May 2024 — Alternative Choice]**

**"Apply Hick's Law and Fitts's Law to compute the interaction efficiency of a mobile e-commerce checkout screen, and recommend two design improvements."**

#### Part B(a) — Hick's Law Application (7 marks)

**Model Answer:**

**1. State the Law and the Given Data (1 mark):**
A mobile checkout screen presents the user with **$n = 6$ payment options** (UPI, Card, Net Banking, Wallet, COD, EMI). The Hick's Law constants are $a = 120 \text{ ms}$ and $b = 180 \text{ ms/bit}$.

**2. Compute Bits of Information (2 marks):**

$$
H = \log_2(n+1) = \log_2(6+1) = \log_2(7)
$$

$$
H = \dfrac{\ln(7)}{\ln(2)} = \dfrac{1.9459}{0.6931} \approx 2.807 \text{ bits}
$$

**3. Compute Decision Time (2 marks):**

$$
T = a + b \cdot \log_2(n+1) = 120 + 180 \times 2.807
$$

$$
T = 120 + 505.26 = 625.26 \text{ ms}
$$

**4. UX Interpretation and Improvement (2 marks):**
The user takes approximately **$625 \text{ ms}$** to decide. **Design Improvement 1:** Apply the **default selection** to UPI (the most popular option), reducing effective $n$ from 6 to 5. **Design Improvement 2:** Group options into two semantic clusters — *"Pay Now"* (UPI, Card, Wallet) and *"Pay Later"* (EMI, COD) — reducing cognitive load to a two-step decision.

#### Part B(b) — Fitts's Law Application (7 marks)

**Model Answer:**

**1. State the Law and the Given Data (1 mark):**
The "Place Order" button has **width $W = 72 \text{ px}$** and is **distance $D = 400 \text{ px}$** from the user's typical thumb position. Fitts's constants: $a = 60 \text{ ms}$, $b = 160 \text{ ms/bit}$.

**2. Compute the Index of Difficulty (2 marks):**

$$
ID = \log_2\!\left(\dfrac{D}{W}+1\right) = \log_2\!\left(\dfrac{400}{72}+1\right)
$$

$$
ID = \log_2(5.556 + 1) = \log_2(6.556)
$$

**3. Evaluate the Logarithm and Movement Time (2 marks):**

$$
ID = \dfrac{\ln(6.556)}{\ln(2)} = \dfrac{1.8795}{0.6931} \approx 2.712 \text{ bits}
$$

$$
MT = a + b \cdot ID = 60 + 160 \times 2.712 = 60 + 433.92 = 493.92 \text{ ms}
$$

**4. UX Interpretation and Improvement (2 marks):**
The user takes approximately **$494 \text{ ms}$** to acquire the target. **Design Improvement 1:** Increase the button **width to $W = 144 \text{ px}$** (double the size). New $ID = \log_2(400/144 + 1) = \log_2(3.778) \approx 1.918$ bits → New $MT \approx 367 \text{ ms}$ (a **$26\%$** improvement). **Design Improvement 2:** Use a **sticky bottom-positioned** "Place Order" bar that follows the thumb, reducing $D$ dramatically during scroll, and apply **high-contrast colour** to leverage *Visibility* (Norman).

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Where Students Commonly Lose Marks in "Kinds of Interaction & UX" Questions:**
>
> 1. **Conflating UI and UX.** Writing "UI is the design of the screen" without explaining UX as the *holistic experience* loses 1–2 marks. UX is defined by **ISO 9241-210** — quote it.
> 2. **Listing interaction styles without examples.** KTU examiners award marks for **real-world examples** (Alexa, MS-DOS, macOS Finder, Wikipedia). Bare definitions get partial credit only.
> 3. **Muddling Shneiderman and Norman.** Shneiderman coined **Direct Manipulation**; Norman coined the **Gulf of Execution / Evaluation** and the **Seven Principles**. Do not mix them up.
> 4. **Skipping math in Hick/Fitts questions.** Even a correct qualitative answer will lose 3+ marks if the **logarithmic computation** is omitted. Show all steps.
> 5. **Not recommending a design improvement.** KTU Part B questions explicitly demand *recommendations* — do not stop at the calculation.
> 6. **Forgetting the Honeycomb facets.** Many students remember only "Usable and Useful" — learn all **seven** facets.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Four Kinds of Interaction (Preece, Rogers & Sharp):** *Instructing, Conversing, Manipulating, Exploring.*
- **Shneiderman's Three Pillars of Direct Manipulation:** *Continuous representation, physical actions, rapid reversible operations with immediate feedback.*
- **Norman's Seven Principles:** *Visibility, Feedback, Constraints, Mapping, Consistency, Affordance, Error prevention/recovery.*
- **Norman's Two Gulfs:** *Gulf of Execution* (user's intent → system action) and *Gulf of Evaluation* (system state → user's perception).
- **Morville's UX Honeycomb (7 facets):** *Useful, Usable, Findable, Credible, Desirable, Accessible, Valuable.*
- **Hick's Law:** $T = a + b \cdot \log_2(n+1)$ — applies to **menu/option selection**.
- **Fitts's Law:** $MT = a + b \cdot \log_2\!\left(\dfrac{D}{W}+1\right)$ — applies to **target acquisition** (buttons, links).
- **UX ≠ UI:** UI is the visual interface; UX is the complete, emotional, end-to-end journey.
- **ISO 9241-210** is the **canonical UX definition** — quote it whenever asked.
- **Accessibility is non-negotiable:** Conversing and Instructing styles are most accessible; Manipulating is least accessible for motor-impaired users.
- **For Part B answers, always end with a *design recommendation*** grounded in a cited law or framework.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: The Basic Process Components of UX
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECIT756 - USER INTERFACE AND USER EXPERIENCE DESIGN
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** The Basic Process Components of UX

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Terminology)

In the **KTU 2024 Scheme** framework for *User Interface and User Experience Design* (PECIT756), **The Basic Process Components of UX** refers to the systematic, iterative sequence of **disciplined activities** that a UX designer/team performs to translate user needs into a usable, useful, and desirable product. The process is not a rigid linear waterfall but a **cyclic, user-centered design (UCD) pipeline** governed by **ISO 9241-210** (Human-Centred Design principles).

The canonical process components (as adopted by the KTU syllabus) are the **four (sometimes five) core pillars** that any UX engagement must traverse:

1. **Understanding / Research Phase** — Empathize, Define, and Discover.
2. **Synthesis / Analysis Phase** — Translate research into actionable insights (Personas, Scenarios, User Stories).
3. **Design / Ideation Phase** — Information Architecture, Wireframing, Interaction Design, Prototyping.
4. **Evaluation / Testing Phase** — Usability Testing, Heuristic Evaluation, Validation.
5. **Iteration / Refinement** — Continuous loop back to earlier stages based on feedback.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Definition (verbatim-grade):**
> *"UX process components are the structured, interrelated set of activities—Research, Design, Prototyping, and Evaluation—executed iteratively to ensure that the resulting product is **useful, usable, desirable, findable, accessible, and credible** (the 6 pillars of UX by Peter Morville)."*

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you are a **chef opening a new restaurant** in Kerala.

- **Research** = You walk around the city, taste competitor food, ask locals (Mappila biryani lovers, appam enthusiasts) what they crave, and you stand in a kitchen watching them cook at home.
- **Synthesis (Analysis)** = You create a *persona*: *"Ramesh, 32, IT professional from Kochi, wants quick, healthy breakfast under ₹100."* This becomes your design brief.
- **Design / Prototyping** = You sketch the menu, the kitchen layout, the table arrangement, and you cook a **test batch** (a low-fidelity prototype) before opening night.
- **Evaluation** = You invite 10 friends, watch them eat, ask "Was the dosa crispy? Was the chutney too spicy?"
- **Iteration** = Based on their feedback, you tweak the recipe, change the lighting, retrain the chef.

**The dish that finally reaches the customer is the result of these 5 components running in a cycle.**

> [!NOTE]
> **Why KTU stresses this topic:** Module 2 of PECIT756 forms the **process backbone** of the entire course. Every later module (UI design, usability metrics, evaluation methods) plugs into this process. KTU examiners frequently ask **"List and explain the basic UX process components"** for 3, 7, and 14-mark questions.

---

## 1.3 Standard UX Metrics & Quality Constants (KTU Recognized)

The following are the **universally accepted, syllabus-mandated metrics** used in the UX process:

- **System Usability Scale (SUS)** — Score range **0–100** (Brooke, 1996).
- **Net Promoter Score (NPS)** — Range **−100 to +100**.
- **Task Completion Rate** — Expressed as **% (0–100)**.
- **Time on Task** — Measured in **seconds (s)**.
- **Error Rate** — Expressed as **errors per task**.
- **Learnability Index** — Difference in SUS score between first and repeated use.
- **Nielsen's 5-Engineer Rule (Heuristic Test)** — Optimal cost-benefit ratio achieved at **3–5 evaluators**.

> [!TIP]
> The **6 Pillars of UX (Peter Morville's Honeycomb)** — **Useful, Usable, Desirable, Findable, Accessible, Credible** — are the **non-negotiable quality gates** the UX process components must satisfy. Memorize them as a hexagonal diagram for KTU board exams.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Five Basic Process Components — Decomposed

Below is the **operational breakdown** of each component, with the *Why* and *How* for every sub-step.

### Component A — **User Research (Empathize & Discover)**

- **Why:** Without research, designers design for *themselves*, not for users. **68% of product failures** are attributed to poor user research (Standish Group).
- **How:**
  - **Qualitative Methods:** Interviews, Contextual Inquiry, Ethnographic Observation, Focus Groups, Diary Studies.
  - **Quantitative Methods:** Surveys, Analytics (Google Analytics, Hotjar, Mixpanel), A/B Testing data, Card Sorting.
  - **Output:** Raw research data, observation notes, audio/video transcripts.

### Component B — **Synthesis & Analysis (Define & Model)**

- **Why:** Raw data is useless until patterns are extracted and a clear problem statement emerges.
- **How:**
  - **Affinity Diagramming** — Group sticky notes by themes.
  - **Persona Creation** — Fictional archetypes representing user segments.
  - **User Stories** — `As a [persona], I want to [action], so that [benefit].`
  - **Empathy Maps** — Says / Thinks / Does / Feels quadrants.
  - **Jobs-To-Be-Done (JTBD)** Framework.
  - **Output:** A **Point-of-View (POV) Statement** and a **Problem Statement**.

### Component C — **Design & Ideation (Sketch & Structure)**

- **Why:** This is where the **information architecture (IA)** and **interaction patterns** are defined.
- **How:**
  - **Information Architecture:** Sitemap, Card Sorting (open/closed), Tree Testing.
  - **Sketching:** Paper wireframes, Crazy 8s, SCAMPER ideation.
  - **Wireframing:** Low-fidelity (Lo-Fi) → Mid-fidelity (Mid-Fi) → High-fidelity (Hi-Fi) using tools like **Figma, Balsamiq, Axure, Adobe XD**.
  - **Interaction Design:** User flows, state diagrams, navigation patterns.
  - **Output:** Wireframes, sitemaps, user flow diagrams.

### Component D — **Prototyping**

- **Why:** A wireframe is *static*; a prototype is *interactive*. Prototypes let users **touch the future product** before code is written.
- **How:**
  - **Low-fidelity prototype:** Paper sketches, clickable PowerPoint/Figma frames.
  - **High-fidelity prototype:** Pixel-perfect interactive mockups in Figma, InVision, Proto.io.
  - **Wizard of Oz:** Human simulates the system behind a curtain.
  - **Output:** A clickable, testable artifact.

### Component E — **Evaluation / Usability Testing**

- **Why:** *"If a UX designer releases a product without testing, they are not a designer — they are a gambler."* — KTU Examiner's adage.
- **How:**
  - **Formative Evaluation** (during design): Heuristic Evaluation (Nielsen's 10), Cognitive Walkthrough, Pluralistic Walkthrough.
  - **Summative Evaluation** (after design): Usability Testing with users, A/B Testing, SUS Survey.
  - **Output:** Severity-ranked bug list, SUS score, redesign recommendations.

### Component F — **Iteration (The Feedback Loop)**

- **Why:** UX is **never finished**. Iteration feeds insights from Evaluation back into Research/Design.
- **How:** **PDCA Cycle** (Plan-Do-Check-Act) or **Double Diamond** model iteration loops.

---

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> **For 14-mark questions, you will be expected to quote these formulas verbatim.**

| Symbol / Term | Formula / Definition | Unit / Range | Engineering Utility |
|---|---|---|---|
| **System Usability Scale (SUS)** | $SUS = 2.5 \times \sum_{i=1}^{10} (x_i - 1)$ for odd items; $SUS = 2.5 \times \sum_{i=1}^{10} (5 - x_i)$ for even items | **0 to 100** (Score ≥ 68 = above average) | Standardized usability score |
| **Nielsen's Severity Rating** | $S = \sum_{i=1}^{n} (F_i \times I_i \times P_i)$ | **0 to 4 scale** | Bug prioritization in heuristic eval |
| **Task Completion Rate (TCR)** | $TCR = \dfrac{T_{completed}}{T_{total}} \times 100\%$ | **% (0 to 100)** | Measures learnability |
| **Task Time Average** | $\bar{T} = \dfrac{1}{n}\sum_{i=1}^{n} T_i$ | **seconds (s)** | Measures efficiency |
| **Error Rate (ER)** | $ER = \dfrac{E_{total}}{T_{total}}$ | **errors/task** | Measures user mistakes |
| **NPS (Net Promoter Score)** | $NPS = \%Promoters - \%Detractors$ | **−100 to +100** | User loyalty |
| **5-Engineer Rule** | $N_{evaluators} = 3 \text{ to } 5$ | **count** | Cost-optimal heuristic test |
| **Coverage (C) in Card Sorting** | $C = \dfrac{C_{placed}}{C_{total}} \times 100\%$ | **% (0 to 100)** | IA quality |
| **Discoverability** | $D = \dfrac{F_{found}}{F_{total}} \times 100\%$ | **% (0 to 100)** | Information findability |
| **Double Diamond** | $\text{Discover} \rightarrow \text{Define} \rightarrow \text{Develop} \rightarrow \text{Deliver}$ | **4 phases** | Process framework |

---

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **IT Industry (Kerala):** Startups like **FreshToHome, Niveus, Kerala IT Parks apps** use the UX process components to retain customers.
- **Healthcare:** The **NEET/JEE entrance portals** in India rely on iterative UX testing to reduce candidate dropouts.
- **Automotive UX:** Tesla's in-car UI uses **iterative prototyping** — over **30+ iterations** before a feature is locked.
- **Banking:** SBI YONO app went through **4 major UX redesigns** between 2017 and 2024 based on usability testing.
- **Defence / Aviation:** Cockpit UI follows **MIL-STD-1472** and **DO-178C** standards, all of which mandate the **5 UX process components** as a regulatory requirement.

> [!TIP]
> In your KTU 14-mark answer, always end with a **"real-world example"** line — examiners reward application-level thinking with **+1 to +2 grace marks**.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Analytical Matrix — Mapping UX Process Components to Design Tools

| UX Process Component | Primary Goal | Key Activities | Standard Tools | KTU Recognized Deliverable |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **1. Research** | Empathize & discover needs | Interviews, Surveys, Field Studies | Google Forms, Otter.ai, Dovetail | Research Report |
| **2. Synthesis** | Convert data to insights | Affinity Diagrams, Personas, Empathy Maps | Miro, Mural, FigJam | Persona Document, POV Statement |
| **3. IA & Wireframe** | Structure information & flow | Card Sort, Sitemap, Sketching | OptimalSort, Whimsical, Balsamiq | Sitemap, Lo-Fi Wireframes |
| **4. Prototype** | Make ideas tangible | Hi-Fi mockups, Interactive flows | **Figma**, Adobe XD, InVision | Clickable Prototype |
| **5. Evaluation** | Validate with users & heuristics | Usability Test, Heuristic Eval | Maze, Lookback, UserTesting | Severity Bug List, SUS Report |
| **6. Iteration** | Refine based on feedback | PDCA, Double-Diamond loop | Issue trackers (Jira, Linear) | Redesign Sprints |

## 3.2 Step-by-Step Derivation — Calculating a SUS Score (Standard KTU Numerical)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The **System Usability Scale (SUS)** is the **most frequently tested numerical** in KTU ESE for PECIT756. A typical 14-mark question may include a sub-part like *"Given the following 10 user responses, calculate the SUS score."*

### Worked Example

A user rates the following 10 statements on a **5-point Likert scale (1 = Strongly Disagree, 5 = Strongly Agree)**:

| # | Statement | Response (x) |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | I think I would like to use this system frequently. | 4 |
| 2 | I found the system unnecessarily complex. | 2 |
| 3 | I thought the system was easy to use. | 4 |
| 4 | I think I would need the support of a technical person. | 1 |
| 5 | I found the various functions in the system were well integrated. | 3 |
| 6 | I thought there was too much inconsistency in the system. | 2 |
| 7 | I would imagine that most people would learn to use this system very quickly. | 4 |
| 8 | I found the system very cumbersome to use. | 1 |
| 9 | I felt very confident using the system. | 4 |
| 10 | I needed to learn a lot of things before I could get going with this system. | 2 |

### Step 1 — Classify the items

- **Odd-numbered items (1, 3, 5, 7, 9):** Positively worded → use **$(x_i - 1)$**.
- **Even-numbered items (2, 4, 6, 8, 10):** Negatively worded → use **$(5 - x_i)$**.

### Step 2 — Compute the contribution of each item

| i | $x_i$ | Type | Contribution |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | 4 | Odd (+) | $4 - 1 = 3$ |
| 2 | 2 | Even (−) | $5 - 2 = 3$ |
| 3 | 4 | Odd (+) | $4 - 1 = 3$ |
| 4 | 1 | Even (−) | $5 - 1 = 4$ |
| 5 | 3 | Odd (+) | $3 - 1 = 2$ |
| 6 | 2 | Even (−) | $5 - 2 = 3$ |
| 7 | 4 | Odd (+) | $4 - 1 = 3$ |
| 8 | 1 | Even (−) | $5 - 1 = 4$ |
| 9 | 4 | Odd (+) | $4 - 1 = 3$ |
| 10 | 2 | Even (−) | $5 - 2 = 3$ |

### Step 3 — Sum the contributions

$$
\begin{aligned}
\sum_{i=1}^{10} C_i &= 3 + 3 + 3 + 4 + 2 + 3 + 3 + 4 + 3 + 3 \\
&= 31
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 4 — Multiply by 2.5 to get the final SUS score

$$
\begin{aligned}
SUS &= 2.5 \times \sum_{i=1}^{10} C_i \\
&= 2.5 \times 31 \\
&= 77.5
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 5 — Interpret the score

> A SUS score of **77.5** is **above the global average of 68** → the system is **considered usable**, and the design passes the **evaluation component** of the UX process. If the score were < 68, the team would **iterate back to the Design/Prototype component** and refine.

---

## 3.3 Worked Example — Severity Rating of Usability Bugs (Heuristic Evaluation)

Suppose a heuristic evaluation of the **KTU Student Portal** finds 3 usability violations:

| Bug | Frequency (F) | Impact (I) | Persistence (P) |
|---|---|---|---|
| Login button hidden on mobile | 4 | 3 | 3 |
| Inconsistent font in footer | 2 | 1 | 2 |
| Form error not auto-scrolling into view | 3 | 4 | 3 |

Severity $S = F \times I \times P$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
S_1 &= 4 \times 3 \times 3 = 36 \quad (\text{Catastrophe — fix first}) \\
S_2 &= 2 \times 1 \times 2 = 4 \quad (\text{Minor — fix last}) \\
S_3 &= 3 \times 4 \times 3 = 36 \quad (\text{Catastrophe — fix first})
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Nielsen's Severity Scale:** 0 = Not a problem, 1 = Cosmetic, 2 = Minor, 3 = Major, 4 = Catastrophe. Any bug with **$S \geq 25$** must be fixed before release.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Flow — The UX Process Components (Cyclic Model)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start: User Need] --> B[Research Phase]
    B --> C[Synthesis and Analysis]
    C --> D[Information Architecture and Wireframing]
    D --> E[Prototyping]
    E --> F[Evaluation and Usability Testing]
    F --> G{Does it meet SUS 68 and 6 Pillars?}
    G -- No --> H[Iterate: Back to Design]
    H --> D
    G -- Yes --> I[Deliver to Engineering]
    I --> J[Post Launch Analytics]
    J --> B
```

> **Reading Guide:** The diagram shows the **non-linear, cyclic nature** of the UX process. Notice the loop-back arrow from **Evaluation → Design** — this is the **iteration component** that students most often forget to mention in 14-mark answers.

---

## 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture — UX Process Components and Their Inputs/Outputs

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph INPUTS
        direction TB
        I1[User Pain Points]
        I2[Business Goals]
        I3[Tech Constraints]
    end

    subgraph PROCESS_COMPONENTS
        direction TB
        P1[COMPONENT 1 Research]
        P2[COMPONENT 2 Synthesis]
        P3[COMPONENT 3 Design and IA]
        P4[COMPONENT 4 Prototype]
        P5[COMPONENT 5 Evaluation]
        P6[COMPONENT 6 Iteration Loop]
    end

    subgraph OUTPUTS
        direction TB
        O1[Personas and POV]
        O2[Sitemap and Wireframes]
        O3[Clickable Prototype]
        O4[Usability Report with SUS Score]
    end

    I1 --> P1
    I2 --> P1
    I3 --> P1
    P1 --> P2
    P2 --> O1
    O1 --> P3
    P3 --> O2
    O2 --> P4
    P4 --> O3
    O3 --> P5
    P5 --> O4
    O4 --> P6
    P6 -.feedback.-> P1
```

> This block diagram is the **canonical answer structure** for a 14-mark question: *"Explain the basic process components of UX with a block diagram."* Always include the **feedback loop** — it differentiates a topper's answer from an average one.

---

## 4.3 Conceptual Map — The 6 Pillars of UX (Peter Morville's Honeycomb)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A[Useful] --> B[Usable]
    B --> C[Desirable]
    C --> D[Findable]
    D --> E[Accessible]
    E --> F[Credible]
    F --> A
```

> The **Hexagonal Honeycomb** is a **one-liner answer** that examiners love. Quote it as: *"A product passes the UX process only if it satisfies all 6 pillars — Useful, Usable, Desirable, Findable, Accessible, Credible."*
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 📘 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. Define the basic process components of UX Design.** *[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The basic process components of UX Design are the **structured sequence of activities** followed to create a user-centered product. According to the KTU 2024 syllabus, the **five core components** are:

1. **User Research** — Understanding user needs, goals, and pain points through interviews, surveys, and field studies.
2. **Synthesis and Analysis** — Converting raw research data into actionable insights using **Personas, Empathy Maps, and POV statements**.
3. **Design and Information Architecture** — Creating **wireframes, sitemaps, and user flows** that structure the product.
4. **Prototyping** — Building **interactive mockups** (lo-fi to hi-fi) so users can test the design before coding.
5. **Evaluation and Iteration** — Conducting **usability testing and heuristic evaluation** to measure the product against **Peter Morville's 6 UX pillars** (Useful, Usable, Desirable, Findable, Accessible, Credible) and iterating based on findings.

*(1 mark for definition + 2 marks for listing the 5 components with one-line descriptions.)*

---

### **Q2. What is a Persona? How is it created during the UX process?** *[KTU University Exam - May 2024]*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

A **Persona** is a **fictional, research-based archetype** that represents a segment of real users. It is a deliverable of the **Synthesis component** of the UX process.

**Creation Steps:**
1. **Collect data** from user interviews and surveys (Research component).
2. **Identify behavioral patterns** — goals, frustrations, motivations.
3. **Group similar users** into 3–5 clusters.
4. **Fictionalize the cluster** by giving a name, photo, age, occupation, and a quote.
5. **Validate** with stakeholders.

A persona typically contains: **Name, Photo, Demographics, Goals, Frustrations, Scenario, and a Quote.**

*(1 mark for definition + 2 marks for creation steps.)*

---

## 📘 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Module Internal Choice)

---

### **❓ Question A (14 Marks) — Choice 1**

**Q. (a)** Explain the **five basic process components of UX design** in detail with a suitable block diagram. **(7 marks)**

**Q. (b)** Differentiate between **formative and summative evaluation**. List and briefly explain **Nielsen's 10 Usability Heuristics**. **(7 marks)**

---

### ✅ Model Solution — Q (a) — 7 Marks

**Introduction (1 mark):**
The UX process is a **user-centered, iterative cycle** that transforms user needs into a usable product. As per **ISO 9241-210** and the KTU 2024 syllabus, the **five basic process components** are:

**Body (5 marks):**
