## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024]**
*Define Flutter. List any four advantages of using Flutter for cross-platform app development.*

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

**Definition (1 Mark):** Flutter is an open-source UI software development kit (SDK) developed by Google that uses the Dart programming language to build natively compiled, cross-platform applications for mobile, web, and desktop from a single codebase.

**Four Advantages (2 Marks — 0.5 each):**
1. **Single Codebase:** One Dart codebase works on Android, iOS, Web, Windows, macOS, and Linux.
2. **Hot Reload:** UI changes appear in under 1 second without losing app state.
3. **High Performance:** AOT compilation produces native ARM code, achieving near-native speed (60–120 FPS).
4. **Rich Widget Library:** Provides Material Design and Cupertino widgets out of the box, reducing reliance on third-party UI libraries.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024]**
*Differentiate between StatelessWidget and StatefulWidget in Flutter with one example each.*

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

| Feature | StatelessWidget | StatefulWidget |
|---------|----------------|----------------|
| Mutability | Immutable after build | Mutable via State object |
| Rebuild | Only when parent rebuilds | Via `setState()` or parent rebuild |
| Example | `Icon(Icons.home)` | `Checkbox`, `Slider`, `TextField` |

(1.5 Marks for the table + 1 Mark for valid examples + 0.5 Mark for concluding statement)

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, Internal Choice)

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)**

**Q3(a). [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Style]**
*Explain the layered architecture of Flutter with a neat diagram. Describe the role of Skia and the Dart VM in the rendering pipeline.* **(7 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**1. Architecture Overview (3 Marks):**
Flutter's architecture is divided into three primary layers:

- **Dart Application Layer:** Where developers write widget-based UI code using the Material/Cupertino libraries.
- **Flutter Engine (C++):** The performance-critical core that contains Skia, the Dart VM, and platform channel APIs.
- **Embedder Layer:** Platform-specific shims (`flutter.jar` on Android, `Flutter.framework` on iOS) that host the engine.

**2. Role of Skia (2 Marks):**
Skia is an open-source **2D graphics rendering engine** written in C++ that Flutter uses to draw every pixel on the screen. When the widget tree updates, Skia receives a scene graph and converts it into OpenGL/Vulkan/Metal commands, which the GPU executes. This eliminates the need for an intermediate browser or OS-specific UI bridge, giving Flutter direct rendering control.

**3. Role of Dart VM (2 Marks):**
The Dart VM (Virtual Machine) executes the Dart code. During development, it operates in **JIT (Just-In-Time) mode** to enable Hot Reload. For production builds, Dart code is **AOT (Ahead-Of-Time) compiled** into native ARM/x64 machine code, which is then executed directly by the device CPU — bypassing the VM entirely for maximum performance.

**Diagram (refer to Section 4.1 of these notes):** *(1 Mark reserved for the labeled architecture diagram)*

---

**Q3(b). [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Style]**
*Write a complete Flutter program to create a simple counter application. The app should display a number in the center of the screen and a Floating Action Button (FAB) at the bottom-right to increment the counter. Explain the role of `setState()`. **(7 Marks)***

**Model Answer:**

**Complete Program (5 Marks):**

```dart
import 'package:flutter/material.dart';

void main() => runApp(const CounterApp());

class CounterApp extends StatelessWidget {
  const CounterApp({super.key});
  @override
  Widget build(BuildContext context) {
    return MaterialApp(
      title: 'KTU Counter',
      debugShowCheckedModeBanner: false,
      home: const CounterScreen(),
    );
  }
}

class CounterScreen extends StatefulWidget {
  const CounterScreen({super.key});
  @override
  State<CounterScreen> createState() => _CounterScreenState();
}

class _CounterScreenState extends State<CounterScreen> {
  int _count = 0;  // (i) State variable

  void _increment() {
    setState(() {   // (ii) Triggers UI rebuild
      _count++;
    });
  }

  @override
  Widget build(BuildContext context) {
    return Scaffold(
      appBar: AppBar(
        title: const Text('KTU Counter App'),
        backgroundColor: Colors.blueAccent,
      ),
      body: Center(
        child: Text(
          'Count: $_count',   // (iii) Displays state
          style: const TextStyle(fontSize: 48, fontWeight: FontWeight.bold),
        ),
      ),
      floatingActionButton: FloatingActionButton(
        onPressed: _increment,   // (iv) Tap handler
        child: const Icon(Icons.add),
      ),
    );
  }
}
```

**Explanation of `setState()` (2 Marks):**

The `setState()` method is a **callback function** provided by the Flutter framework that performs two critical operations:
1. It **mutates the state variable** (`_count++`) inside its closure.
2. It **marks the corresponding widget as "dirty"** and schedules a rebuild of that widget and its descendants in the **next frame** (within 16ms for 60 FPS).

**Without `setState()`**, even if the variable changes, the UI will not reflect the new value because Flutter has no signal that a rebuild is required. This is the central principle of Flutter's **reactive programming model**.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**

**Q4(a). [KTU University Exam — June 2024 Style]**
*Describe the step-by-step procedure to set up the Flutter development environment and create a new Flutter project. List the key files and folders generated by `flutter create`.* **(7 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**Step 1 — Install Flutter SDK (1 Mark):**
Download the Flutter SDK from the official Flutter website and extract it to a directory. Add the `bin` subfolder to the system `PATH` environment variable.

**Step 2 — Install Supporting Tools (1 Mark):**
- Install **Android Studio** (for Android SDK, emulator, and platform tools).
- Install **VS Code** with the Flutter and Dart extensions (recommended IDE).
- Install **Java Development Kit (JDK 17+)** required by Android.

**Step 3 — Verify Installation (1 Mark):**
Run `flutter doctor` in the terminal. Resolve all red `❌` errors before proceeding.

**Step 4 — Create Project (2 Marks):**

$$
\text{flutter create ktu\_flutter\_app}
$$

This generates the project structure.

**Step 5 — Key Files & Folders (2 Marks):**

| File/Folder | Purpose |
|-------------|---------|
| `lib/main.dart` | Entry point; contains the `main()` function and root widget |
| `pubspec.yaml` | Project metadata, dependencies (like `http`, `provider`) |
| `android/` | Android-specific Gradle configuration, manifest, icons |
| `ios/` | iOS-specific Xcode project, Info.plist, assets |
| `test/` | Unit and widget test files |
| `web/`, `macos/`, `linux/`, `windows/` | Optional platform folders |

**Step 6 — Run the App (1 Mark — bonus):**
Connect a device or launch an emulator, then run `flutter run`.

**Q4(b). [KTU University Exam — June 2024 Style]**
*Explain the concept of the Widget Tree in Flutter. How does the framework reconcile (diff) the old and new widget trees when state changes? Why is this approach efficient?* **(7 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**1. Widget Tree Concept (2 Marks):**
The Widget Tree is the **hierarchical structure of all widgets** that compose the UI of a Flutter app. The root widget (typically `MaterialApp`) sits at the top, with child widgets (`Scaffold`, `AppBar`, `Text`, etc.) nested below. The tree mirrors the visual nesting on the screen — a `Container` containing a `Row` containing two `Text` widgets produces three levels of nesting.

**2. Reconciliation / Diffing Algorithm (3 Marks):**
When `setState()` is called, Flutter builds a **new widget tree** and compares it with the **old widget tree** using the **Element Tree** as an intermediary:

- **Step A:** Flutter walks both trees simultaneously.
- **Step B:** If a widget in the new tree has the **same `runtimeType` and `key`** as the old one, Flutter **updates the existing Element in place** (cheap operation).
- **Step C:** If the **type or key differs**, Flutter **defuncts (unmounts) the old Element** and **mounts a new one** (expensive operation).
- **Step D:** Only the **dirty branch** of the tree is rebuilt — unaffected widgets are skipped entirely.

**3. Why This is Efficient (2 Marks):**
- **Minimal Repaints:** Only changed regions are repainted, not the entire screen.
- **Constant Keys Enable Reordering:** Developers can use `Key` objects to preserve state when list items are reordered.
- **O(N) Diffing:** For typical UIs, the diff is linear in the number of widgets, ensuring smooth 60 FPS performance.

**Diagram (refer to Section 4.4):** *(Reserved for the reconciliation flow diagram)*

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks in KTU Exams:**
>
> 1. **Forgetting `setState()`** — Students often mutate the variable directly (`_counter++` outside `setState`). The UI won't update, and the examiner will deduct 2–3 marks.
> 2. **Confusing `Hot Reload` with `Hot Restart`** — Hot Reload preserves state; Hot Restart does not. Examiners frequently test this distinction.
> 3. **Writing "Flutter uses Java"** — Flutter uses **Dart**, NOT Java. This is a guaranteed 1-mark penalty.
> 4. **Omitting the `build()` method** — The `build(BuildContext context)` method is **mandatory** in every widget. Forgetting it is a compilation error.
> 5. **Saying Flutter is "made by Apple" or "made by Facebook"** — Flutter is developed by **Google**. Confusing it with React Native (Facebook) costs marks.
> 6. **Missing the `super.key` constructor parameter** — Modern Flutter requires `const MyWidget({super.key});` for lint compliance.
> 7. **Using `runApp(MyApp())` without `const`** — While not a bug, omitting `const` in `runApp(const MyApp())` is a performance penalty that examiners notice.

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

- ✅ **Flutter = Google's open-source UI SDK** using **Dart** language.
- ✅ **Single codebase → 6 platforms** (Android, iOS, Web, Windows, macOS, Linux).
- ✅ **"Everything is a Widget"** — UI, layout, padding, alignment are all widgets.
- ✅ **Two widget types:** `StatelessWidget` (immutable) and `StatefulWidget` (mutable).
- ✅ **`setState()` is mandatory** for any UI update inside a StatefulWidget.
- ✅ **Architecture layers:** Dart App → Flutter Engine (Skia + Dart VM) → Embedder.
- ✅ **Skia** = 2D graphics engine for pixel rendering.
- ✅ **AOT compilation** in release mode = native ARM code, near-native performance.
- ✅ **JIT compilation** in debug mode = enables Hot Reload (< 1 sec refresh).
- ✅ **`flutter doctor`** = environment diagnostic command.
- ✅ **`flutter create <name>`** = scaffolds a new project.
- ✅ **`flutter run`** = builds and deploys to connected device/emulator.
- ✅ **60 FPS** target = 16.67ms per frame budget.
- ✅ **Element reconciliation** diffs old and new widget trees for efficient rebuilds.
- ✅ **`MaterialApp`** = root widget providing Material Design theme.
- ✅ **`Scaffold`** = provides `AppBar`, `body`, `FloatingActionButton`, `drawer`.
- ✅ **Hot Reload preserves state; Hot Restart resets it.**
- ✅ **Widget Tree** = hierarchical, nested representation of the UI.
- ✅ **Used in production by Google Pay, BMW, eBay, Alibaba** (real-world validation).

> [!TIP]
> **Final KTU Exam Mantra:** *"Widget, setState, build, Skia, AOT, Hot Reload."* If you can confidently explain these six terms with examples, you are fully prepared for any question on this topic.
